While writing a custom backend for django-registration, I've had the requirement to extend the provided `RegistrationProfile` with additional fields to request from a user. For example, maybe you require knowing the user's `company` or `location`.

After a couple days of working, I have a very custom solution that works for my django project. It required:

* subclass `registration.models.RegistrationProfile` and `registration.models.RegistrationManager` in order to add fields to the `RegistrationProfile` and account for them in `RegistrationManager.create_inactive_user`
* subclass `registration.admin.RegistrationAdmin` in order to expose the new fields on `RegistrationProfile`. This mostly copy-and-pasted code.
* subclass `registration.forms.RegistrationForm` in order to add the additional `RegistrationProfile` fields and expose them during the registration process.
* subclass `registration.backends.default.views.RegistrationView` in order to override the `form_class` specifying my custom form as well as overriding `RegistrationView.register` in order to be able to pass the additional `RegistrationProfile` fields through to `create_inactive_user`.
* copy-and-paste the `registration.backends.default.urls` *exactly*, only overriding the imports for my custom views instead of the default backed views.
* remove `registration` from my project's `settings.py`, specifying my custom django-registration "backend application" instead, since at this point it's more of a custom django application than an overriding django-registration backend.

After doing this work, I started to think it would make sense to refactor the base views in `registration` as well as the provided backend views in order to allow the model being used to be more "pluggable". Specifically, follow the implementation of the `form_class` attribute in `registration.views.RegistrationView`, allowing the author of the custom backend to optionally specify a custom `RegistrationProfile`; a custom model.

I'm still thinking through potential side effects to backwards compatibility and project settings (for example my last bullet above mentioning the changes to my project's `settings.py`).

Is this something the project would benefit from? Does my thought process at least make sense? I'm going to spend some time working on this, but would love any feedback or ideas.

issue_title: Merge enhancement: Check last position in bisect
issue_body:
As possible performance boost one could check the last position first in the bisect-helper function.

Why this may be faster:

- The last item in `current` is the next one to be yielded. The bisect is called every time the next item of the iterable from which the yielded item came from is to be inserted in `current`.
- If the `iterables` are somewhat consecutive the next item may be (with high probability) the next item to be yielded again.
- If there are few `iterables` the normal bisect and the altered bisect will behave quite similar.
- If there are lots of `iterables` doing one more comparison before starting the bisection will not slow it down unnecessary.

These assumptions are somewhat debatable, I haven't tested it!

issue_title: ReferenceFields validate_<field> method not called and instead ValidationError raised
issue_body:
I am using a ReferenceField in my mongoengine Document. The `get` method for the view works absolutely fine. But while creating a new object i.e. on a `POST` i am getting a validation error for the field `DBRef invalid dereference.`

For other fields where I needed to validate or modify the values I generally used `validate_<field>` method to get the raw value and then modify/change it accordingly and then return it. But in this case the validate_<field> method is not being called and ValidationError is being raised before that.

I tried digging into the code and found that following is something that DRF does:

```
        for field in fields:
            validate_method = getattr(self, 'validate_' + field.field_name, None)
            primitive_value = field.get_value(data)
            try:
                validated_value = field.run_validation(primitive_value)
                if validate_method is not None:
                    validated_value = validate_method(validated_value)
```

according to which `validate_<field>` method is called after calling `field.run_validation`

DRFM's referencefield is extended using `rest_framework.serializers.Field` class which calls `self.to_internal_value` in `run_validation` method which then raises the error in the following code:

```
    def to_internal_value(self, data):
        try:
            dbref = self.model_field.to_python(data)
        except InvalidId:
            raise ValidationError(self.error_messages['invalid_dbref'])
``` 

what I would as a user want to do is modify the raw (json) data that I get on a post request in the `validate_<field>` method, get the reference object (filtering it by the raw data that I have received) if it exists and return it or raise a validation error there. Currently this is not possible for the reference field in DRFM.

Match logic of ModelField.

In particular: kwarg `model_field` should be added/removed automagically inside `get_field_kwargs`.

Also, ObjectField should be scalar and not DocumentField

issue_title: Parameter server always reserves 1 GPU as long as TensorFlow GPU build is used
issue_body:
Hello, 

In the `start_cluster_server` method in `TFNode.py`:

```
if tf.test.is_built_with_cuda():
    # GPU
    gpu_initialized = False
    retries = 3
    while not gpu_initialized and retries > 0:
      try:
        # override PS jobs to only reserve one GPU
        if ctx.job_name == 'ps':
          num_gpus = 1

issue_title: executesql() causes IndexError if only colnames is used
issue_body:
When calling executesql('<some query\>', colnames=["something.something"]), the code causes an IndexError because it tries to access an empty list. 

> Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/srv/www/web2py/gluon/shell.py", line 274, in run
    execfile(startfile, _env)
  File "/srv/www/web2py/applications/iadev/scripts/test_auction_get_ia116h.py", line 402, in <module>
    test_values_private()
  File "/srv/www/web2py/applications/iadev/scripts/test_auction_get_ia116h.py", line 135, in test_values_private
    rand_user['id'], filters={"private": True})
  File "applications/iadev/modules/auction.py", line 1094, in get_running_sql
    query, user_bids_only = self._build_running_query_sql(user_id, filters)
  File "applications/iadev/modules/auction.py", line 925, in _build_running_query_sql
    colnames=["auction_id"])
  File "/srv/www/web2py/gluon/packages/dal/pydal/base.py", line 821, in executesql
    data, fields=extracted_fields, colnames=colnames)
  File "/srv/www/web2py/gluon/packages/dal/pydal/adapters/base.py", line 305, in parse
    for row in rows
  File "/srv/www/web2py/gluon/packages/dal/pydal/adapters/base.py", line 224, in _parse
    tmp = tmps[j]
IndexError: list index out of range

The tmps[] variable is created in function `_parse_expand_colnames()` in `web2py/gluon/packages/dal/pydal/adapters/base.py,` but as it iterates over fields, none is created (`len(tmps) == 0`)
```python
def _parse_expand_colnames(self, fieldlist):
    ...
    for field in fieldlist:
        ...
        tmps.append(...)
    ....
    return (..., tmps)
```

The value is then accessed by iterating over colnames in `_parse()`, but since `len(tmps) == 0 and len(colnames) >= 1`, the code will cause an `IndexError`, as it will try to access non-existing value.
```python
def _parse(self, row, tmps, fields, colnames, blob_decode,
           cacheable, fields_virtual, fields_lazy):
    ...
    for (j, colname) in enumerate(colnames):
        ...
        tmp = tmps[j]  # causes IndexError here
        ....
```

Having only fields value set and not colnames works, as colnames variable is generated from fields values if not set (None), in `web2py/gluon/packages/dal/pydal/base.py` function `executesql()`

```python
def executesql(self, query, placeholders=None, as_dict=False,
               fields=None, colnames=None, as_ordered_dict=False):
    ...
    if fields or colnames:
        ...
        if not colnames:
                colnames = [f.sqlsafe for f in extracted_fields]
        ....
```

This issue was caused by commit https://github.com/web2py/pydal/commit/5cd99f57816edec300a1974e9db396d1345deb2c and is present in current version (both release and latest repository version)

When running example/image-classification/benchmark_score.py with the GoogLeNet symbol, it causes a crash (see  #4827):
`src/operator/./pooling-inl.h:208: Check failed: param_.kernel[0] <= dshape[2] + 2 * param_.pad[0] kernel size (7) exceeds input (6 padded to 6)` 

In short, the error occurs because layer `pool6` attempts to pool with a 7x7 kernel on a 6x6 input. 

The cause is MXNet's default `"valid"` pooling convention, which causes `pool1`, `pool3`, `pool4`, and `pool5` to produce smaller output matrices than expected (one element less per row and per column), eventually resulting in above error. 


The correct symbol definition should be:

```
def get_symbol(num_classes = 1000, **kwargs):
    data = mx.sym.Variable("data")
    conv1 = ConvFactory(data, 64, kernel=(7, 7), stride=(2,2), pad=(3, 3), name="conv1")
    pool1 = mx.sym.Pooling(conv1, kernel=(3, 3), stride=(2, 2), pool_type="max", pooling_convention='full') #added pooling_convention
    conv2 = ConvFactory(pool1, 64, kernel=(1, 1), stride=(1,1), name="conv2")
    conv3 = ConvFactory(conv2, 192, kernel=(3, 3), stride=(1, 1), pad=(1,1), name="conv3")
    pool3 = mx.sym.Pooling(conv3, kernel=(3, 3), stride=(2, 2), pool_type="max", pooling_convention='full') #added pooling_convention
    
    in3a = InceptionFactory(pool3, 64, 96, 128, 16, 32, "max", 32, name="in3a")
    in3b = InceptionFactory(in3a, 128, 128, 192, 32, 96, "max", 64, name="in3b")
    pool4 = mx.sym.Pooling(in3b, kernel=(3, 3), stride=(2, 2), pool_type="max", pooling_convention='full') #added pooling_convention
    in4a = InceptionFactory(pool4, 192, 96, 208, 16, 48, "max", 64, name="in4a")
    in4b = InceptionFactory(in4a, 160, 112, 224, 24, 64, "max", 64, name="in4b")
    in4c = InceptionFactory(in4b, 128, 128, 256, 24, 64, "max", 64, name="in4c")
    in4d = InceptionFactory(in4c, 112, 144, 288, 32, 64, "max", 64, name="in4d")
    in4e = InceptionFactory(in4d, 256, 160, 320, 32, 128, "max", 128, name="in4e")
    pool5 = mx.sym.Pooling(in4e, kernel=(3, 3), stride=(2, 2), pool_type="max", pooling_convention='full') #added pooling_convention
    in5a = InceptionFactory(pool5, 256, 160, 320, 32, 128, "max", 128, name="in5a")
    in5b = InceptionFactory(in5a, 384, 192, 384, 48, 128, "max", 128, name="in5b")
    pool6 = mx.sym.Pooling(in5b, kernel=(7, 7), stride=(1,1), pool_type="avg")
    flatten = mx.sym.Flatten(data=pool6)
    fc1 = mx.sym.FullyConnected(data=flatten, num_hidden=num_classes)
    softmax = mx.symbol.SoftmaxOutput(data=fc1, name='softmax')
    return softmax
```

issue_title: [Android,v0.11.0] The result of mxnet_predict is wrong! AND how to compile the amalgamation in Android Studio?

issue_body:
I have the following case. Assume that I have have an NDArray object, A, with the shape (D, dim) where dim is any arbitrary shape and D is the minibatch size. I have another NDArray object, B, with the shape (d, dim) where d < D. Now, I have a mapping between the axis-0 indices of B and A, such that entry B[i] is mapped to A[j] where j >= i. My aim is to add each B[i] to A[j]. The naivest thing I could to is to iterate on B's axis0 and do for each B[i] the operation A[j] += B[i]. But each such addition operation is costly. To reduce the it, I keep the track of each consequent j entries in the A object and do addition as A[j1:j2] += B[i1:i2]. While this lowers the number of additions, still I have to do multiple ones.

I wonder if such an operation is possible currently: Let's say I know all the j indices, [j1,j2, ..., jd]. In numpy, it is possible to do operations by using the index array as A[[j1,j2,...,jd]] += B. Trying the similar operation results in "ValueError: NDArray only support continuous slicing on axis 0" with MxNet NDArray. My purpose is to execute this addition in a single command. Is this possible with the current MxNet API?

Hi!

I noticed that zmq.ssh.tunnel.openssh_tunnel does not fail when it should. If I just call:

```python
import time

import zmq
import zmq.ssh

ctx = zmq.Context()
publisher = ctx.socket(zmq.PUB)

ssh_tunnel_pid = zmq.ssh.tunnel_connection(
    socket=publisher,
    addr="tcp://127.0.0.7:43777",
    server="localhostA", # mind the typo
    password=None,
    keyfile=None)

time.sleep(10)
```

there will be no error. However, on line 230 in zmq/ssh/tunnel.py:

```python
tunnel = pexpect.spawn(cmd, env=env)
```
, the value of `cmd` is: `ssh  -f -S none -L 127.0.0.1:47209:127.0.0.7:43777 localhostA sleep 60`

If I execute this command in my bash, I get: `ssh: Could not resolve hostname localhosta: Name or service not known`

by default, when tasks from luigi.contrib.redshift have `RedshiftTarget` as an output, they do not set the port. e.g. in RedshiftQuery:


`    

    def output(self):
        """
        Returns a RedshiftTarget representing the executed query.
        Normally you don't override this.
        """
        return RedshiftTarget(
            host=self.host,
            database=self.database,
            user=self.user,
            password=self.password,
            table=self.table,
            update_id=self.update_id
        )
`

RedshiftTarget is based on PostgresTarget.
So if your redshift host doesn't include the port, these Tasks will fail, because they will default back to 5432. 
so if your Redshift instance is running on the default port, your tasks will fail until you explicitly override the output method, passing the port to the RedshiftTarget constructor.

Not a massive issue, for sure, but for consistencies sake, this ought to be changed.

issue_title: bucket name with "." not DNS compatible, connect timeout
issue_body:
env: python(2.7), boto3 (1.2.3), botocore (1.3.22)

I want to upload a file to a bucket named 'mybucket.com' in Tokyo, but it always raised ConnectTimeout error. I enabled the debug mode and it shows that 'Not changing URI, bucket is not DNS compatible: auto3d.test'.

I checked the source code in botocore/utils.py and fount this comment in method check_dns_name:

    """
    Check to see if the ``bucket_name`` complies with the
    restricted DNS naming conventions necessary to allow
    access via virtual-hosting style.

    Even though "." characters are perfectly valid in this DNS
    naming scheme, we are going to punt on any name containing a
    "." character because these will cause SSL cert validation
    problems if we try to use virtual-hosting style addressing.
    """

So, at present I can't manage bucket with '.' ? Or is there any other better way to manage this bucket in python? 

BTW, I used this bucket as a static website, so I can't rename the bucket without '.' like 'mybucket'. I just want a python script to help me deploy web files easily.

Thanks.

issue_title: Make warnings only fail the build during development
issue_body:
In #52 and #54, we made the compilation step fail if there were any warnings.

This is great for the detection of potential issues during development and PR review.

However, it is not great for production releases. `ciso8601` is a source distribution, so each user compiles it with each installation. Users with exotic compiler configurations would have the installation of ciso8601 fail, because their compiler might be pickier about what it warns about.

Make `ciso8601` only treat warnings as errors during development and PR review, and not during release installations.

issue_title: Bug in GETTEXT  when loading templates dynamically
issue_body:
I'm using **jinja2** templates dynamically in pyramid. It works fine for basic usage, but it fails when trying to load internationalized text with the GETTEXT() function:

```
@view_config(context=Test)
def test_view(request):
    #Render templates dynamically:
    template = "template1.jinja2"
    return Response(render(template, {'test1':1}, request=request))
```

Basic jinja2 template:

```
<!-- template/template1.jinja2 -->
{{ GETTEXT('NAME') }} - {{ test1 }}
```

Throws the following error:

```
File "/python3.4/site-packages/pyramid/i18n.py", line 218, in get_localizer
    return request.localizer
AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'localizer'
```

I've tracked the error,  it seems to be a problem on file **pyramid_jinja2/i18n.py** when the function **localizer()** is calling **i18n.get_localizer(get_current_request())**, but the  function **get_current_request()** generates a **None** value. So it's not being able to create a valid Request.

```
class GetTextWrapper(object):

   ....

    @property
    def localizer(self):
        return i18n.get_localizer(get_current_request())

   ....
```

issue_title: Options to improve Python runtime autodiscovery on Windows
issue_body:
Pipenv currently has two Python runtime discovery and/or installation strategies:

1. Search `PATH` (in `pipenv.cli.find_a_system_python`), adding any `pyenv` environments to the end before it does so
2. Install it with `pyenv` (in `pipenv.cli.ensure_python`)

This misses a few potential possibilities for improvement on Windows:

1. Discovery based on the Windows launcher configuration file (https://docs.python.org/3/using/windows.html#customization)
2. Discovery based on the Windows registry (https://github.com/zooba/pep514tools#usage)
3. Supporting `conda` based automatic Python installation in addition to `pyenv` installation
4. Supporting @uranusjr's https://github.com/uranusjr/pythonup-windows (and its macOS counterpart)

Actually doing this would probably requiring refactoring the autodiscovery code though, such that the discovery & installation strategy used by `ensure_python` can be chosen at import time based on the platform.

issue_title: Treq / requests GET params behaviour difference on None values
issue_body:
While trying to port some code from requests to treq, I've encountered a weird compatiblity issue when a GET param has a None value. When using requests a None value is not sent. Meaning that

```python
requests.get("http://server.example.com/", params={"foo": None})
```

actually sends a `GET / HTTP/1.1`, while the same kind of code with treq 
```python
treq.get("http://livna.org", params={"foo": None})
```
sends `GET /?foo=None HTTP/1.1`

Looking at the requests code, the "params" item is processed through the requests.sessions.merge_setting function, which removes None values.

I'd like to think treq should have the same behaviour.

issue_title: Document how to handle cycles in Topology
issue_body:
In streamparse 3.2 it doesn't look like there is first class support for cycles as there was with the clojure dsl.

If you are to attempt to define a cycle in the naive way, you will reference a variable before it is defined because declaration of inputs happens at class instantiation time. _this works for all graphs that can be topologically sorted_:
```python
	word_spout = WordSpout.spec()
	count_bolt = WordCountBolt.spec(inputs={word_spout: Grouping.fields('word')}, par=1)

	a_bolt = A.spec(name="A", inputs=[count_bolt["count->A"], b_bolt["B->A"]]) # here this fails because b_bolt is not defined
	b_bolt = B.spec(name="B", inputs=[c_bolt["C->B"]])
	c_bolt = C.spec(name="C", inputs=[a_bolt["A->C"]])
```

The current solution therefore is to init each bolt and then extend inputs:
```python
def extend_inputs(bolt, inputs):
	additions = bolt._sanitize_inputs(inputs)
	bolt.inputs = {**additions, **bolt.inputs} # only python >3.5
	bolt.common = ComponentCommon(inputs=bolt.inputs, streams=bolt.outputs, parallelism_hint=bolt.par, json_conf=bolt.config)

class WordCount(Topology):
	word_spout = WordSpout.spec()
	count_bolt = WordCountBolt.spec(inputs={word_spout: Grouping.fields('word')}, par=1)
	a_bolt = A.spec(name="A", inputs=[count_bolt["count->A"]])
	b_bolt = B.spec(name="B")
	c_bolt = C.spec(name="C")

	extend_inputs(a_bolt, [b_bolt["B->A"]])
# and so on...
```
Is there a better way to do this currently?

issue_title: manylinux1 wheels compiled against too new numpy version
issue_body:
scikit-image claims to require numpy >= 1.7.2. However, for scikit-image 0.12.3 the manylinux1 wheels were built against a newer numpy. This then leads to runtime errors like "ValueError: numpy.dtype has the wrong size, try recompiling" or "RuntimeError: module compiled against API version 9 but this version of numpy is 7". I tested this against the x64 wheels in Python 2.7, 3.3., 3.4, and 3.5 with numpy 1.7.2. It fails for all those versions except 3.4, which is a bit weird, so I assume this particular wheel was built against an older numpy.

As a proof, have a look at this build log: https://travis-ci.org/neothemachine/rawpy/jobs/132485700#L1660 This is for building my own manylinux1 wheels for my own library in a docker container, so maybe you want to test this in a simpler setup, just installing an old numpy version and trying to install and run scikit-image from a manylinux1 wheel in different Python versions.

I posted code and pictures here:
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/32785495/skimage-pyramid-guassian-not-working-as-expected

I'm doing everything like I've seen in examples so I'm thinking it's not a user error?

Here I list a serie of possible removals for make the nupic repo clearer and easy to follow and maintain as discussed in ML. Some of these removals should also be aplied to nupic.core in case of equivalent code (numenta/nupic.core#354).

* * *

`nupic/research`:
- [X] Dead code (`DataGenerator.py`, `ObjDiff.py`, `TrainingHooks.py`, `TrainingHooks.py`, `anomalyzer.py`). These dead codes are used in anywhere (except in their tests/examples). Issue: https://github.com/numenta/nupic/issues/1857, Fixed by: https://github.com/numenta/nupic/pull/1858
- [ ] Obsolete implementations of SP (`FDRCSpatial2.py`, `flat_spatial_pooler.py` and `fdrutilities.py`): Do we really need this old implementation as `spatial_pooler` is its replacement?. Another point, `fdrutilities.py` has a lot of dead code. Issue: https://github.com/numenta/nupic/issues/1864, Fixed by: https://github.com/numenta/nupic/pull/1872
- [X] Derived implementations of TP (`TPTrivial.py` and `TrivialPredictor.py`): I've checked the use of these classes in code, and I found that are little used. Issue: https://github.com/numenta/nupic/issues/1865, Fixed by: https://github.com/numenta/nupic/pull/1873
- [X] Vision code. Issue: #1863 

It seems that TP is the big problem to a clean repository as it has several derived classes and hacks to keep consistency between them. As `temporal_memory` gets mature, we could remove TP and its inheritance and so the repository finally would get nice and clean. By the way, while TP is not fully removed, some derived classes could be removed right now (`TPTrivial`, `fast_temporal_memory`, etc).
Part of #2000

issue_title: Update readme install instructions for Linux & package nupic.bindings-latest
issue_body:
The readme instructions for Linux (not OSX because that does `pip install nupic` only) have to be updated each time the `nupic.bindings` version is bumped. I suggest packaging 2 packages on each bindings release: 
* the versioned one (as so far)
* "latest" (I've seen it's a common to use pip version `999` to indicate the latest release), this is a convenience placeholder
* "upstream"  (`666` version :smiling_imp: ) on each PR merge to `.core`, w/o waiting for the version bump.

issue_title: Add a programatic way to find out if a settings write was successful
issue_body:
Currently if a `SBP_MSG_SETTINGS_WRITE` fails in firmware, it just spits out an error log.

This does not provide a robust way for a program or script to determine if a setting was successfully written.

We need add a `SBP_MSG_SETTINGS_WRITE_RESP` that:
- returns a status code to indicate success, or an error (and a unique code for each type of failure to help a programmer find which field was bad)
- returns the new setting value if successful (or the old value if it failed), this means a tool can check for any roundtrip problems (e.g. UART corruption)
`datetime` currently throws a circular reference when attempting to be dumped. This should ideally be built into the library and support iso formats for serialization and deserialization
I pass `addon_after="%"` when creating a form field (with `layout="horizontal"`, if that matters), and no addon appears.

There are a few limitation with the flush method on send streams, particularly in Python:
- There is only a blocking version, no asyncio version.
- The blocking version does not release the GIL, so it cannot even be made to behave asynchronously by running it on a separate thread.

One could make an asynchronous version purely in Python, because the asyncio stream keeps track of the number of outstanding heaps already.

I want to quickly say first Djoser is a great package to kickoff DRF REST API projects and I've used it to its absolute maximal intent and beyond. Great job!

I figure I would add my 2cents on some improvements though. Well, actually it's just one, the rest I found very flexible and inline with OOP/DRY.

With respect to sending e-mails, I would look into decoupling it from the DRF Views completely.
Say for example you have attachments (which is not so uncommon), with Djoser as is, I had to make some minor but ugly modifications to Djoser and email views I sub-classed.

Currently Djoser does something similar with Email as TemplateView (or more generally, class based views) in Django, in that there is a "get_context_data" method etc...that is passed in to render the text (the e-mail subject, body etc...) given the path to the template (subject.html, body.html etc...).

This isn't bad, in most cases, but again, if I have to deal with attachments, and also with different types of scenarios (send to multiple recipients, bccs etc...), this may not be the best solution.

Instead of coupling that to the DRF Views, I would decouple it completely and anytime Email functionality is needed, simply reference an Email class within the code and call it.

Here is simple email class I wrote (I called it mixin class by accident) I'm using currently in one of my projects with djoser:

https://github.com/apokinsocha/django-email-mixin/blob/master/django-email-mixin.py

It maybe a bit redundant with respect to djangos existing email function, but I wanted to encapsulate some additional common functionality with respect to e-mails.

Usage example:

``` python
    _file = some_file
    html_body = DjangoEmailWrapper.get_template_text('email_body.txt', context=context)
    subject = DjangoEmailWrapper.get_template_text('subject.txt', context=context, inline=True)
    emails = ['test@gmail.com', 'test2@gmail.com']
    msg = DjangoEmailWrapper(subject, getattr(settings, 'DEFAULT_FROM_EMAIL', None), html_body, bcc=emails, html_body=html_body)
    msg.attach_file(_file)
    msg.send_email()
```

I can do a PR in the near future to integrate it, if there is foreseeable use beyond my own.
A pretty simple change in my view, but I think it cleans things up a bit and also allows to easily call DjangoEmailWrapper in other non-djoser views.

issue_title: JWT_AUTH_HEADER_PREFIX Changes Authorization Header but Not WWW-Authenticate
issue_body: `JWT_AUTH_HEADER_PREFIX` is not being used when [constructing the `WWW-Authenticate` header](https://github.com/GetBlimp/django-rest-framework-jwt/blob/df2a01c48dc0d7b804577cb9f94863b54a32d8de/rest_framework_jwt/authentication.py#L104). Currently that value is hardcoded to `JWT`.

why am I not seeing them with the python call?

## Steps to reproduce

Because the steps to reproduce include `sudo apt-get install`, I have placed
them in a Vagrantfile. Vagrant makes it trivial to create and destroy virtual
machines that have the same environment every time. This is useful for
reproducible development environments, but also to demonstrate bugs.

If you do not want to use Vagrant, look for the code below.

      config.vm.provision "shell", inline: <<-SHELL
        # ... this is the code you need ...
      SHELL

### Vagrant usage

* Install Vagrant from [https://www.vagrantup.com]()
* Create a directory, and place the contents below in a file called `Vagrantfile`
* `cd` to the directory and run `vagrant up`
* The problem mentioned above will occur at the end of the provisioning.
* You can use `vagrant ssh` to SSH into the virtual machine
* You can use `vagrant destroy` + `vagrant up` to recreate the machine from scratch

### Vagrantfile

Call this file `Vagrantfile`. See the previous section for usage.

    # -*- mode: ruby -*-
    # vi: set ft=ruby :

    # All Vagrant configuration is done below. The "2" in Vagrant.configure
    # configures the configuration version (we support older styles for
    # backwards compatibility). Please don't change it unless you know what
    # you're doing.
    Vagrant.configure("2") do |config|

      config.vm.box = "bento/ubuntu-16.04"

      config.vm.synced_folder "data", "/vagrant_data"

      config.vm.provision "shell", inline: <<-SHELL
        # apt-get update

        sudo apt-get install -y \
          build-essential \
          mercurial \
          git \
          virtualenv \
          virtualenvwrapper \
          python2.7 \
          python-dev \
          libfreetype6-dev \
          libjpeg-dev \
          libav-tools \
          libsdl-image1.2-dev \
          libsdl-mixer1.2-dev \
          libsdl-ttf2.0-dev \
          libsmpeg-dev \
          libsdl1.2-dev \
          libportmidi-dev \
          libswscale-dev \
          libavformat-dev \
          libavcodec-dev \
          libfreetype6-dev \
          unzip \
          zlib1g-dev

        . /usr/share/virtualenvwrapper/virtualenvwrapper.sh
        mkvirtualenv kivy
        kivypip=$HOME/.virtualenvs/kivy/bin/pip

        $kivypip install cython==0.25
        $kivypip install kivy==1.10.0
      SHELL
    end

issue_title: Receives yahoo historical csv data in zip form, so read_csv fails for zipped data
issue_body: For some reason, urlopen returns data from yahoo in a zip form. this could be due to internet service providers adding additional headers in the request to bump up bandwith. Because of this compressed data received from yahoo, the read csv function in data.py fails. It might be worth adding a check before read_csv if the data received is in zip (gzip) form and unzip it before reading? Please advice.

Here's a buffer test:

>>> x='jim' + 100*' ' + 'my'

10371

'\x1bh\x00\x00\x8c\x94n\xe6\xa2lS\x03\xa9\xb4\xbc\x92\x04\xb4@\xf6\x89ree\x00_BrotliBuildHistogramsWithContext\x00_BrotliEstimateBitCostsForLiterals
(a bunch more garbage)
\x00_BrotliEncoderInputBlockSize\x00_BrotliEncoderWriteData\x00enc/././metablock.h\x00_BrotliEncoderCompressStream\x00_BrotliEncoderCompress\x00_BrotliEncoder'

200

>>> brotli.decompress(z)

'\x1bh\x00\x00\x8c\x94n\xe6\xa2lS\x03\xa9\xb4\xbc\x92\x04\xb4@\xf6\x89ree\x00_BrotliBuildHistogramsWit'

issue_title: Potential buffer over-reads due to incorrect masking
issue_body:
When building Chromium with the v1.0.0 version of Brotli using the VC++ /analyze feature I see the following warnings:

brotli\enc\compress_fragment_two_pass.c(461) : warning C6385: Reading invalid data from 'cmd_histo':  the readable size is '512' bytes, but '1024' bytes may be read.
brotli\enc\compress_fragment_two_pass.c(474) : warning C6385: Reading invalid data from 'cmd_bits':  the readable size is '256' bytes, but '512' bytes may be read.
brotli\enc\compress_fragment_two_pass.c(474) : warning C6385: Reading invalid data from 'cmd_depths':  the readable size is '128' bytes, but '256' bytes may be read.
brotli\enc\compress_fragment_two_pass.c(475) : warning C6385: Reading invalid data from 'kNumExtraBits':  the readable size is '512' bytes, but '1024' bytes may be read.
brotli\enc\static_dict.c(90) : warning C6385: Reading invalid data from 'kBrotliDictionarySizeBitsByLength':  the readable size is '25' bytes, but '128' bytes may be read.

Manual examination of these warnings suggest that they are all valid. In all cases an index is being anded with a bit mask but the bit mask has too many set bits.

In the first four cases a bit mask of 0xff is used by the arrays only have 128 elements. It would be safer to define a COUNTOF macro and change the code like this:

    ++cmd_histo[commands[i] & 0xff];

to something like this:

    ++cmd_histo[commands[i] & (COUNTOF(cmd_histo) - 1)];

Or, just change the constant to 0x7f, or use a named constant to make sure they stay in sync.


The fifth warning is a bit different because the index is being masked down to 0-127 but the array is 25 elements long. This seems odd. If the value of 'len' is guaranteed to be in range then the masking is not needed, and if isn't guaranteed to be in range then the masking is insufficient. Maybe there is other data in the high bits of 'len' - I can't tell. The safest thing to do, it seems, would be to pad kBrotliDictionarySizeBitsByLength out to 32 bytes (at a cost of six bytes of storage) and then mask with 0x1f instead of 0x7f.

issue_title: Implement pandas.describe sufficiently minimal for DAI stats
issue_body:
A Frame object should be able to provide:

- [x] The number of unique values in a column (~~#856~~, ~~#858~~)
- [x] The most frequent item in a column (the mode) (~~#860~~, ~~#863~~)
- [x] The number of occurrences of the most frequent item (~~#860~~)

NAs are not included. If more than one item shares the spot for most frequent item, then either one can be returned.
As with the other statistics, all results should be returned in a Frame.

```
>>> d = dt.DataTable([1, 2, 3, 3, 2, 3, 1, 3, 2, None])

>>> d.nunique().topython()
[[4]]
>>> d.mode().topython()
[[3]]
>>> d.modefreq().topython()
[[4]]
```
Function names are subject to change.

issue_title: Support for message methods such as CopyFrom, MergeFrom and SerializePartialToString
issue_body:
Currently pyrobuf does not support some of the various protobuf message methods such as `CopyFrom`, `MergeFrom` and `SerializePartialToString`. Rather the create a rather large pull-request without warning, I thought I'd create this issue so that the changes could be discussed beforehand.

The changes can be seen in [my fork](https://github.com/grgsrs/pyrobuf).

I've done my best to keep the performance as fast as possible. However, the deserialization performance has noticeably dropped. On my machine serialization takes on average 0.634480 seconds with pyrobuf 0.5.4 and 0.592283 seconds after my changes. Deserialization takes on average 0.416573 seconds with pyrobuf 0.5.4 and 0.632511 seconds after my changes.

One reason for the drop in performance is that I've modified the message validation code to check sub-messages as-well. Pyrobuf's `ParseFromString` method does validate a message after deserialization, while Google's protobuf does not, so performance could be improved by dropping message validation in `ParseFromString` (my guesstimate is that this would cut deserialization time by approximately 0.06 seconds).

I ran across this library [while searching for a library to provide me with fs locking mechanism to run program once](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/380870/python-single-instance-of-program).

However, the script uses `__del__` method, which is incorrect: https://github.com/pycontribs/tendo/blob/master/tendo/singleton.py#L71 

From [python docs](https://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#object.__del__): 

> It is not guaranteed that __del__() methods are called for objects that still exist when the interpreter exits.

Which would cause it to be locked forever.

##### TLDR;
This is not really a "code bug", but rather a potential misusage issue with the "BitOpNot" operator. The "BitOpNot" does exactly what it supposes to do (which is flipping the bit). However, since we are using bitmapist as a stat tool, we expect it to give us the result of negation of a population set, which "BitOpNot" does not provide. 

##### Example 
Suppose you have total of 100 users. You use bitmapist to mark active user with event "active". Assume you have 5 active user and the bitmap data look like this: 
```
1011011
```

Now you want to count how many active user you have
```
print len(MonthEvents('active', now.year, now.month)) 
#print 5, correct number.
```

Now suppose you wanna know how many inactive user you have. 
```
print len(BitOpNot(MonthEvents('active', now.year, now.month)))  
#print 2. 
```

Here the negation of the "active user set" gives us size of 2 instead of 95. 

The fundamental problem is that the variable length bitmap data only contain information about "who are active", but it does not contain information about the population size.

issue_title: Reactived Users should be able to log in
issue_body:
When a user account is inactivated by the AccountExpiryBackend, in most cases the admins will want to re-activate the users. They will do that by going into the Admin Site, set is_active to True and save the user.

However, the last_login of the User is still an old date, so when the just re-activated user tries to log in they are again inactivated by the same AccountExpiryBackend.
Users should be able to log in after an admin re-activated their account.

The same thing happens when the User fails to enter the right password LOGIN_FAILURE_LIMIT times and it is inactivated because of that.
The admin page for LoginAttemptAdmin has an Activate link that will reset the failed logins counter, but the admin is expected to know that the user will have to be re-activated using the link in that page.
It would be nicer to let the admin activate users in the normal way and reset the counter on re-activation instead.

issue_title: [spec] Example table GDEF gives two caret positions for glyph f_i
issue_body:
[Section 9.b](http://www.adobe.com/devnet/opentype/afdko/topic_feature_file_syntax.html#9.b) of the OpenType Feature File specification gives an example for `table GDEF`. Currently, it contains the following statement:  
```
LigatureCaretByPos f_i 400 380;
```

However, for an `f_i` glyph, there should probably be just a single caret position. Should the example be changed to some glyph with three components, perhaps f_f_l?

issue_title: Treat AppEngine apps as a "first-class citizen" for gcloud.storage.acls ?
issue_body:
(This is in reference to https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/object-change-notification#_Application_Example)

In the example where you can set up notifications, I stumbled across the following command for gsutil:

```gsutil acl ch -u ApplicationId@appspot.gserviceaccount.com:FC gs://BucketName```

In this example, we're effectively saying "Give ApplicationId (an AppEngine application) full control (FC) to the bucket called BucketName".

This made me realize that we have "users" (which is what this uses), "groups", and "domains".. Can we add an `.app()` method (not married to that name... maybe `.appengine()`)? It would effectively be an alias for `.user()` but appends "@appspot.gserviceaccount.com". This way, my code would look like:

```python
bucket = get_bucket(...)
bucket.acl.app('my-appengine-app').grant_read()
```

Currently we have to do...

```python
bucket = get_bucket(...)
bucket.acl.app('my-appengine-app@appspot.gserviceaccount.com').grant_read()
```

and we have to know that magical suffix (which I don't think is really well documented).

Thoughts?

I solved a bug issue for Google Cloud in Python by editing in wsgi_server.py --- Do you know where can i send the patch? I've never sent a patch to Google before. Anyways, Here it goes... I was unable to bind to localhost:8000. The online answers never helped me. Then, i entered into this path:- C:\Program Files\Google\Cloud SDK\google-cloud-sdk\platform\google_appengine\google\appengine\tools\devappserver2 Then, i edited wsgi_server.py There was error in lines 314 and 336 I changed it to:- line 314:- if port != 8000: line 336:-assert port != 8000 After changing it... i did a run on one demo file to check it --- dev_appserver.py guestbook It worked. issue_title: bigquery.ArrayQueryParameter should be able to accept structs as elements of the array
issue_labels: [{'id': 82126549, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/api:%20bigquery', 'name': 'api: bigquery', 'color': 'c0c0c0', 'default': False}, {'id': 544604244, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/priority:%20p1', 'name': 'priority: p1', 'color': 'ffa03e', 'default': False}, {'id': 544604235, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/status:%20acknowledged', 'name': 'status: acknowledged', 'color': 'fef2c0', 'default': False}, {'id': 77438580, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/type:%20bug', 'name': 'type: bug', 'color': 'db4437', 'default': False}, {'id': 77438582, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/type:%20enhancement', 'name': 'type: enhancement', 'color': '8bc34a', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
I [commented](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/pull/2901#discussion_r94066868) that setting the types on array query parameters needs to be recursive since the array could contain structs. Note that [arrays cannot contain arrays](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/reference/standard-sql/data-types#array-type) (the workaround is to contain a struct which can then contain an array).

It's not clear how one would do this with the current `ArrayQueryParameter` constructor.

```
bigquery.ArrayQueryParameter(
    name='myarray', 
    type='STRUCT',  # Note: this isn't enough information to construct the type object.
    values=?  # What would go here? Dictionaries? That doesn't match other uses of StructQueryParameter.
)
```

Maybe we need to change how array query parameters are constructed? Maybe it should look more like the StructQueryParameter constructor?

```
bigquery.ArrayQueryParameter(name, *sub_params)
```

It'd be a pain for scalar values and the names would have to be ignored on the `sub_params` if provided, but it's more flexible.

*An aside*

*I'd actually love if we could get rid of all the `XQueryParameter` classes and use lists, dicts, and ints, floats, etc. But I see that it might be ambiguous for [some types](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/reference/standard-sql/data-types) especially `DATETIME` (no time zone) vs `TIMESTAMP` (stored as UTC time).*

issue_body: Each session holds onto a reference to its "active" transaction. If the application commits or rolls back that transaction explicitly (rather than via our APIs), it should be unlinked from the session.

issue_title: Missing "Using the API" section for Cloud Storage
issue_body:
I'd like a page that tells you how to get started with the API.

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/247555/24724049/95f89b8e-19fe-11e7-9a14-e7f9f3e71800.png)

I know I can go to https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/reference/libraries#client-libraries-usage-python but most people who arrive on the page won't know that.

Could you add a parameter to the `Table.insert_data` method that accepts a partition label? 

I'm trying to write a Python app that is able to copy data from one partition in a table to another partition. I have a data field `timestamp` in my Bigquery schema and I want to query off of this value. However the partition does not always align with values in this field. Some entries with `_PARTITIONTIME 2016-11-11` actually have a timestamp `2016-11-10`. This is usually caused by application delay, latency, or Bigquery outages such as the one on [Tuesday](https://status.cloud.google.com/incident/bigquery/18022#5651442522128384). 

Using the `bq` shell api I am able to use table decorators to target the partition directly. In the below shell example I am able to target a partition using `--destination_table` and the query can limit it's select using a similar partition decorator.  

```shell
bq --project foo-dev query --allow_large_results --destination_table 'my_ds.table_name$20161110' --noflatten_results --append_table 'SELECT * from [my_ds.table_name$20161111] WHERE timestamp BETWEEN TIMESTAMP(\'2016-11-10\') AND TIMESTAMP(\'2016-11-11\')'
```

However in this api it's strange that I have to create 2 table instances, one to address the table (unpartitioned) and one to insert to the table. See below. 

```python
table = dataset.table(TABLE_NAME)
assert not table.exists()
table.create(client=client, dataset=dataset)
table.insert_data(rows, client=client) # will only insert to the current partition
partition = dataset.table(dataset + '.' + TABLE_NAME + '$20161111')
partition.insert_data(rows=rows, client=client) # should insert to the 20161111 partition
```

The `Table.insert_data` source code seems to just pass the table name to the underlying REST api. So assuming the REST api will accept and use the table partition decorator then the code above should insert as I expect. The api would be easier to use for my use case if it handled a partition label if one is provided at the time of insert.

issue_title: Cloud Spanner Client should prevent nested transactions
issue_labels: [{'id': 539494117, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/api:%20spanner', 'name': 'api: spanner', 'color': 'cd7f32', 'default': False}, {'id': 544604244, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/priority:%20p1', 'name': 'priority: p1', 'color': 'ffa03e', 'default': False}, {'id': 544604235, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/status:%20acknowledged', 'name': 'status: acknowledged', 'color': 'fef2c0', 'default': False}, {'id': 486182624, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/status:%20release%20blocking', 'name': 'status: release blocking', 'color': 'ffee58', 'default': False}, {'id': 77438582, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/type:%20enhancement', 'name': 'type: enhancement', 'color': '8bc34a', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
Cloud Spanner does not support [nested transactions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nested_transaction). But the client library does allow users to call `database.run_in_transaction` inside of the callback that is provided to an outer `database.run_in_transaction`. This is misleading since users might believe that this will behave like a nested transaction but in reality these will be two independent transactions. This is confusing and a source of bugs. Specifically the inner transaction might succeed while the outer might ABORT, in which case the callback will be rerun thus rerunning the inner transaction. 
We should prevent this from happening by detecting and raising an error if someone calls `database.run_in_transaction` inside the callback provided to `database.run_in_transaction`.  Note that this needs to be done before Beta since this is a known breaking change.
Also note that if we come across a use case where we do want to allow calling a nested `database.run_in_transaction`, we should be able to enable that in future without making a breaking change.

issue_title: Unable to set StringValue on ObjectField in DataPipeline
issue_body:
I'm unable to properly set a StringValue inside of an ObjectField resource when creating a DataPipeline. 

This error gets returned from CloudFormation:
```
Value of property StringValue must be of type String
```

Snippit of troposphere code:
```
        schedule = PipelineObject(
            Fields=[ObjectField(
                Key='type',
                StringValue=['Schedule']
            ), ObjectField(
                Key='occurences',
                StringValue=['2']
            ), ObjectField(
                Key='startAt',
                StringValue=['FIRST_ACTIVATION_DATE_TIME']
            ), ObjectField(
                Key='period',
                StringValue=['1 day']
            )],
            Id= 'S3_Schedule',
            Name='S3Schedule'
        )
```

I believe this is due to @philtay's latest commit (https://github.com/cloudtools/troposphere/commit/a1957fb04118ce13f2cb37a52bc93718eed0ae41), which now expects StringValue to be an array. 

If I specify the StringValue as a string and not an array, troposphere fails to build the cloudformation as well:
```
TypeError: StringValue is <type 'str'>, expected [<type 'basestring'>, <class 'troposphere.Ref'>]

I could, however, be declaring the ObjectField's completely wrong.

issue_title: Precompute relevant TaskNode for each SelectNode
issue_body:
[Experiments show](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/compare/master...twitter:stuhood/engine-memoize-selectnode) that memoizing this catches about half of the cases where we would otherwise create subgraphs of Noops, and improves the total runtime by about 12%.

The reason memoization only catches half the cases is that we execute the graph breadth-first, so multiple attempts to compute the value have already begun by the time we record the memoized solution.

Computing this up front would mean splitting out (or reimplementing) the walking of `Selectors` to recursively determine viable TaskNodes to compute a particular SelectNode.

cc @ttim @baroquebobcat

issue_title: Depending on a 3rd-party go target in the gen step?
issue_body:
I made good progress with my gRPC integration. However I am stuck figuring out how to express a binary dependency in the `grpc.gen` step.

The proto compiler `protoc` uses plugins (which are executables) to generate code. The plugin for golang lives in `github.com/golang/protobuf/protoc-gen-go` and is usually installed using

    go get -u github.com/golang/protobuf/protoc-gen-go

Given `$GOPATH/bin` is on your `PATH` `protoc` will find it and use it.

With pants, I was able to create a dependency on `protoc-gen-go` by creating `3rdparty/go/github.com/golang/protobuf/BUILD` containing:

~~~
go_remote_libraries(
  rev='2bba0603135d7d7f5cb73b2125beeda19c09f4ef',
  packages=['protoc-gen-go']
)
~~~

Which I can `./pants compile` which puts the binary in `.pants.d/compile/go/3rdparty.go.github.com.golang.protobuf.protoc-gen-go/bin/protoc-gen-go`. 

Here are my problems: 

1) How can I instruct pants to build this target before running the `grpc.gen` step? I tried adding it to `synthetic_target_extra_dependencies`, but this is used later in the pipeline. I have a hunch that I have to express this in `subsytem_dependencies`, but I do not know how and did not find an example of building a target that is defined in the same repo.

2) How can I get programatically the path where the `protoc-gen-go` binary is put by pants? I need to pass this to the `protoc` compiler so it can find the plugin.

issue_title: [pantsd] watchman startup for a large repo can lead to slower than expected clean-all times
issue_body:
in our monorepo, running a `PANTS_ENABLE_PANTSD=True ./pants clean-all` can take around 6 seconds longer than a non-daemon clean-all in the `kill-pantsd` workunit:

```
16:18:55 00:00     [kill-pantsd]
...
DEBUG] terminating pantsd
DEBUG] sending signal 15 to pid 59446
DEBUG] successfully terminated pid 59446
DEBUG] purging metadata directory: /Users/kwilson/dev/source/.pids/pantsd
DEBUG] Selected watchman binary bootstrapped to: /Users/kwilson/.cache/pants/bin/watchman/mac/10.12/4.5.0/watchman
DEBUG] terminating watchman
DEBUG] sending signal 15 to pid 59444
DEBUG] sending signal 9 to pid 59444
DEBUG] successfully terminated pid 59444
DEBUG] purging metadata directory: /Users/kwilson/dev/source/.pids/watchman
16:19:01 00:06     [clean-all]
```
it would appear that:

1) because of the size of our repo, the initial watchman crawl takes around ~30s.
2) until its completed with the crawl, it doesn't respond to `kill -15`.
3) this leads the process termination code to send a `kill -15`, wait for the process to die up until a timeout, then send a `kill -9`.
4) the slowness comes from waiting for the ignored `kill -15` to timeout.

this can be improved, but isn't strictly critical for the beta. maybe a nice to have.

issue_title: Make 3rdparty source roots a first-class thing
issue_body: Right now we treat 3rdparty like "source" (in the sense of "source" vs "test"), but it should be a third kind of source root, a sibling concept to source and test. This will make things like GoBuildgen work without hacks.

issue_title: [minor-perf] Don't regenerate class dir lookup table for each compile entry's analysis discovery
issue_body:
`jvm_compile` currently regenerates a class_dir -> compile_context map of all targets involved in the compile for each invalid target to compile in the `_upstream_analysis` method.

It would be better if we only generated it once for the whole compile. It's a global lookup table, so there's no reason to regenerate it each time.

issue_title: GoBuildgen is lossy when re-generating non-gloabally in `--materialize` mode.
issue_body:
As reported by @dudeche:

For example, suppose a repo with many `go_binary` targets, some of which have various dependencies on aws/aws-sdk-go.  If `buildgen.go` is run against only a subset of those targets, the full dependency set on [aws/aws-sdk-go](https://github.com/aws/aws-sdk-go) may not be represented leading to improper dependency thinning, like so:
```
[10:27:04] danbenson@ObvsMBP36:[~/workspace/medium/mono-test]: git diff 3rdparty/golang/github.com/aws/aws-sdk-go/BUILD
diff --git a/3rdparty/golang/github.com/aws/aws-sdk-go/BUILD b/3rdparty/golang/github.com/aws/aws-sdk-go/BUILD
index aa80119..530937a 100644
--- a/3rdparty/golang/github.com/aws/aws-sdk-go/BUILD
+++ b/3rdparty/golang/github.com/aws/aws-sdk-go/BUILD
@@ -3,10 +3,6 @@

 go_remote_libraries(
   packages=[
-    'aws',
     'aws/credentials',
-    'aws/credentials/ec2rolecreds',
-    'service/dynamodb',
-    'service/dynamodb/dynamodbattribute',
   ]
 )
```

Either GoBuildgen needs to be non-destructive for 3rdparty deps or it needs to always operate globally - at least for 3rdparty deps.

issue_title: Pants pythons_tests broken by sloating dep on pytest-timeout / pytest-timeout release.
issue_body:
Just out: https://pypi.python.org/pypi/pytest-timeout/1.0.0 and it says
```json
"run_requires": [
        {
            "requires": [
                "pytest (>=2.8.0)"
            ]
        }
    ],
```

Whereas [pants says](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/release_0.0.58/src/python/pants/backend/python/tasks/pytest_run.py#L71-L80):
```python
class PytestRun(TestTaskMixin, PythonTask):
  _TESTING_TARGETS = [
    # Note: the requirement restrictions on pytest and pytest-cov match those in requirements.txt,
    # to avoid confusion when debugging pants tests.
    # TODO: make these an option, so any pants install base can pick their pytest version.
    PythonRequirement('pytest>=2.6,<2.7'),
    PythonRequirement('pytest-timeout'),
    PythonRequirement('pytest-cov>=1.8,<1.9'),
    PythonRequirement('unittest2', version_filter=lambda py, pl: py.startswith('2')),
    PythonRequirement('unittest2py3k', version_filter=lambda py, pl: py.startswith('3'))
  ]
...
```

This causes all pants installs to start failing to run python tests via action at a distance.
For aurora:
```
$ ./build-support/jenkins/build.sh
...
11:22:51 00:00   [test]
11:22:51 00:00     [run_prep_command]
11:22:51 00:00     [test]
11:22:51 00:00     [pytest]
11:22:51 00:00       [run]
                     
11:23:04 00:13         [chroot]
11:23:11 00:20   [complete]
               FAILURE

Exception message: Could not satisfy all requirements for pytest>=2.6,<2.7:
    pytest>=2.6,<2.7, pytest>=2.8.0(from: pytest-timeout)

```

issue_title: ISO 639-3 code tables now contain a BOM and must be read accordingly
issue_body: Since ISO 639-3 code tables - as of 2018-01-23 - in UTF-8 now contain a BOM, they must [be read](https://github.com/clld/clldutils/blob/0d85c0bf46184bb99c6800ecbfa7f5db87cb2e7e/src/clldutils/iso_639_3.py#L50) using the `utf-8-sig` encoding.

issue_body:
I have a video that has several streams (video + audio + embedded image).

When I try to iterate on frames with:

    video = imageio.get_reader(filepath)
    for frame in range(video):
      # ...
The resulting frames are garbage images and I get :
`Warning: the frame size for reading (213, 120) is different from the source frame size (1916, 1076).`

I believe the problem comes from [that code](https://github.com/imageio/imageio/blob/29fe381dcb9ca16f973be95d93443247c172bd7d/imageio/plugins/ffmpeg.py#L497-L518) which does very unreliable parsing of `ffmpeg`'s output, and for this video file picks another stream's size as the "reading size".

Why not using `ffprobe` (part of FFmpeg) and and its structured output, that is easier to parse, more reliable, and would provide more readable code?

Example:

    import json, subprocess

    cmd = ("ffprobe", "-loglevel", "warning", "-of", "json", "-show_format", "-show_streams", filepath)
    output = subprocess.check_output(cmd)
    metadata = json.loads(output)
    first_video_stream = next(filter(lambda x: x["codec_type"] == "video", 
                                     metadata["streams"]))
    size = (first_video_stream["width"], first_video_stream["height"])

I can help implement the change if you agree.

issue_title: Sorting slices in a dicom series in _dicom.py
issue_body:
Hello,

Firstly, I want to thank you guys for your contribution. Great great library!  

I am looking to use/adapt the code in _dicom.py for reading and working with dicom volumes. One of things I noticed is that the slices that belong to an image series are sorted according to InstanceNumber tag. But from what I have read, it is a type 2 element and not always reliable (e.g. https://stackoverflow.com/questions/32681865/same-instance-number-for-two-dicom-image-in-a-single-series). If slice positions/neighborhoods are what we are interested in, then I think the right way to sort would be based off of their positions in the patient co-ordinate system (like described here https://itk.org/pipermail/insight-users/2003-September/004762.html). I would like to hear your thoughts on this. If you agree with the above, I have a written a function to sort based off of the distance of the slices from the origin, which I can share. 

Thanks
Krishna

Dear imageio community.

I would like to resume my initial effort to package `imageio` for Debian, now that the project has gained some maturity. I intend to centralize all questions, concerns and feedback on the process in this issue, which I'll close once the packaging is achieved or dropped.

So far I have a working build but fail to run the test suite properly. Our builders do not face the internet so fetch remote sources is a no-no. I tried setting the `IMAGEIO_NO_INTERNET=1` envvar, but the testsuite fails with quite a few `InternetNotAllowedError` errors. Perhaps I am doing something wrong here.

Another concern is that the packaged `imageio` should be using the packaged dependencies for freeimage, ffmpeg and other libraries called via ctypes. I guess there is no other way but patch the source code directly, unless I am missing something.

Thanks for your assistance.

Ghis

issue_title: Enable explicit disabling of default plugins
issue_body:
## Summary

In latest changes in #2013, we re-enable default plugins at a version change.

This could be annoying for users who have explicitly disabled a plugin for a reason, so it would be helpful to explicitly blacklist a default plugin to protect against this behaviour.

We should add a session timeout so that when multiple students share a tablet, they don't share progress. Let's choose a reasonable default for now. Maybe 4 hours?

We can explore making it configurable by the admins in a future release.

issue_title: add kolibri command to change the location of the content folder
issue_body:
### Observed behavior

On a number of occasions, we've gotten feedback that folks want the content folder in a different location than the primary Kolibri installation. This often occurs even after a user has imported content, which means that we may need to migrate existing content to the new locations.

### Expected behavior

We should provide a new command to migrate the content directory to a new location. One possibility might be something like:

```bash
kolibri content directory  # displays current directory
kolibri content /media/external  # migrates the content to a new directory
```

However any command is fine if it serves the general purpose.

It would be reasonable to place some restrictions on this command if helpful, e.g. that the Kolibri server can't be running or be started while it's running.

This may also require some additional checks during Kolibri startup, since now there would be a chance that Kolibri might start without the content directory being available, or the content directory might become unavailable while Kolibri is running. These cases might require errors to get bubbled up to the end-user on the front-end.

### User-facing consequences

Users are not able to set up their systems and leverage higher-capacity hard drives as desired

### Observed behavior

There are some inconsistent patterns in the wild, so wanna ensure that our logging is done consistently!

 * Because logging has to be configurable
 * Because the origin of log messages is super critical

Hi

Calling `setup()` in `setup.py` twice is weird and confuses pip.

One example is if you try to use wheels. We use wheels to speed up our Travis CI - our `requirements.txt` is fetched and compiled once, and future builds are about 10 minutes quicker (lots of dependencies!). Unfortunately, because `setup()` is called twice this no longer works:

  running install
  running install_lib

  error: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: 'build/bdist.linux-x86_64/wheel/pysimplesoap/__init__.py'
  ----------------------------------------
  Failed building wheel for pysimplesoap
```

I recreated this locally, and fixed it by only calling `setup()` once.

Another weird thing is that if you `pip install pysimplesoap` in an empty virtualenv and do `pip freeze` you get:

```
$ pip freeze
PySimpleSOAP==1.16
soap2py==1.16
```

That's because `setup.py` is executed by pip during the install. So pip downloads `pysimplesoap` but `setup.py install` will install `pysimplesoap` and `soap2py` from that `.tar.gz`. Of course, depending on which one you install, the other won't have the correct `installed_files.txt` in its egg-info, so `pip uninstall` gets weird:

```
$ pip uninstall soap2py
Uninstalling soap2py-1.16:
  lib/python2.7/site-packages/pysimplesoap
  lib/python2.7/site-packages/soap2py-1.16-py2.7.egg-info
Proceed (y/n)? n

$ pip uninstall pysimplesoap
Uninstalling PySimpleSOAP-1.16:
  lib/python2.7/site-packages/PySimpleSOAP-1.16-py2.7.egg-info
  lib/python2.7/site-packages/pysimplesoap/__init__.py
  lib/python2.7/site-packages/pysimplesoap/__init__.pyc
  lib/python2.7/site-packages/pysimplesoap/c14n.py
  lib/python2.7/site-packages/pysimplesoap/c14n.pyc
  <snip>
  lib/python2.7/site-packages/pysimplesoap/xmlsec.py
  lib/python2.7/site-packages/pysimplesoap/xmlsec.pyc
Proceed (y/n)? n
```

And if you uninstall one of these it will leave the `egg-info` directory for the other.

And if someone else then installs your requirements.txt that you made with `pip freeze` then pip will install `pysimplesoap` which will install both, then `soap2py` which will install both. Fun times!

There are a couple of alternatives you could try:

 * Dummy project for the old name that just depends on the new name and has no other code, with a `README.rst` that gets shown on pypi to explain further.
 * Dummy project for the old name that just explodes when setup.py is run (with a message saying what the new name is). Same README as above.

issue_title: Feature Request: Add docker cli cmd interface to docker-py
issue_body:
It would be quite useful to have docker-py accept a user's command line (just like would have been typed on a bash prompt) and be able to run the equivalent api calls in docker-py. I know this is quite a task and not easily implemented. I just wonder if other people think it would be a good idea.

LVM implements something similar in its liblvm library via an [lvm2_run()](https://git.fedorahosted.org/cgit/lvm2.git/tree/tools/lvm2cmd.h#n77)  function. Example [here](http://osdir.com/ml/linux.lvm.general/2004-03/msg00222.html): `r = lvm2_run(handle, "vgs --noheadings vg1");`

Please feel free to close if this is outside the scope of the library.

issue_title: Index-handling in filter functions and other feedback
issue_body:
Hello devs, first of all I'd like to thank you for your work on this small but promising library. I'm currently wrapping parts of your library to use with a command line tool intended for particle tracking at my university. Thanks to your great example section and generally well documented API that process is very easy and satisfying. 

However I also encountered some minor hiccups and annoyances. Maybe you find my feedback in that regard useful:

- Using `pandas.DataFrame` as the primary data container in your library is a choice I'm not sure about and certainly not used to when comparing trackkpy to other scientific libraries. 
  - I don't understand the index-handling for the functions in `filtering.py`. During grouping the index is dropped (why not move the index back to into a column?) and later "frame" is explicitly set as the index. In that last step the keyword `drop=False` is moved which doesn't make sense because any would-be index was dropped during grouping? Is there a reasoning behind this?
To extend on this, wouldn't it be clearer if all functions treated dataframes and indexes the same way: e.g. leave index untouched or at least be explicit about it in the docstring. I was initially confused several times why this or that function chose to set an index. To me that often seemed arbitrary and not intuitive. 
  -  I also think in many cases the docstrings could be more explicit about the required columns and especially the structure of returned dataframes. I think this is a disadvantage when using `pandas.DataFrame` as the primary data container: it obfuscates the in- and output compared to simple arrays provided through multiple well documented arguments. Maybe a type declaration like `df : pandas.DataFrame[frame, particle, x, y]` could be used within the docstrings to make this clearer.
- Your example on parallelized feature location got me thinking why you don't support something equivalent in `trackpy.batch`. Even multi-threading can speed up this processing step for large sets by magnitudes and can easily be accomplished without depending on `ipyparalell`. E.g.:

```python
from functools import partial
from multiprocessing.pool import ThreadPool

def batch_threaded(frames, locate_kw, threads=4, report_hook=None):
    if not report_hook:
        def report_hook(): pass
    func = partial(trackpy.locate, **locate_kw)
    with ThreadPool(threads) as pool:
        stats = []
        for i, frame_stats in enumerate(pool.imap(func, frames)):
            frame_stats["frame"] = i
            stats.append(frame_stats)
            report_hook()
    return pd.concat(stats, ignore_index=True)
```

- Although calculating the displacement of linked particles between consecutive frames isn't hard I think a function doing that might be an useful addition to this library. Would you agree?

I'm aware that this criticism is subjective and any these are only minor points. So please take this as well meant feedback. Of course I would be happy to work on these points myself if help is desired! :slightly_smiling_face:

from unittest import TestCase
from mock import Mock, call
from mockextras import stub, Store

class Foo:

    def foooo(self, paramX):

class FooTest(TestCase):

issue_title: fix(controller): generate_app_name should re-roll on duplicate name
issue_body: Lurking through old comments, I found https://github.com/deis/deis/issues/475#issuecomment-32907618. If we find an existing app name when generating a random app name, we should re-roll `generate_app_name()` until we find a new name for the application, otherwise we will hit a 500 error because the application name already exists.

Currently, the platform doesn't have the ability to support long-running sessions. To support features on the open roadmap like interactive `deis run` (#117) and `deis logs -f` (#465), we'll need to create a new Go-based component that is responsible for creating long-lived websocket sessions.

issue_title: deisctl gets to "failed/failed" on a service, and then hangs.
issue_body:
On occasion, if I get a service to exit unexpectedly, then deisctl will hang on any subsequent commands.

Example:

```
$ deisctl stop builder
deis-builder.service: failed/failed
(hangs)
```

With a little bit of debugging code in `fleet/stop.go`, I can see that it's stuck in the for loop there:

```go
		lastSubState = currentState.SystemdSubState
		fmt.Printf("Waiting for state %s, but in state %s\n", desiredState, currentState.SystemdSubState) // Added
		time.Sleep(250 * time.Millisecond)
```

Which prints this out every 250 msec:

```
Waiting for state dead, but in state failed
```

I'm not sure if we're legitimately waiting for something to happen, or if `failed` or `dead` should be treated like a special case.

Right now `--sync-level` is required and defaulting to `mtime`. Is this what we want?

Web app defaults to sync "off" but if turned on, the initial selection is `checksum`.

There is no CLI command for creating a shared endpoint.

I don't even quite know where this should go in the CLI command structure.
`globus transfer endpoint share`?
`globus transfer endpoint create --shared`?

If it's a behavior of `endpoint create`, that makes argument handling there much more complex, but I think it might have better feeling semantics once you figure out what command you want.

issue_title: Rename Service Clients to not conflict with OAuth2 usage of the word "Client"?
issue_body:
As I've started working on some more thorough tutorial documents, the word "Client" is a sticking point.

Often, when talking about Auth concepts, we end up focused on things like the `client_id` and having to involve the language of OAuth2 Clients.
That seems inherently confusing when we've been calling the service adapters `TransferClient`, `AuthClient`, etc.

Is there an alternative, suitable name?
One option is to simply name them by the services that they represent, so it becomes

`TransferClient` -> `GlobusTransfer`

and

`AuthClient` -> `GlobusAuth`

But we would presumably keep the OAuth2-oriented subclasses of `GlobusAuth` named by the type of "OAuth2 Client" that they represent.

Is this just a recipe for more confusion?

issue_title: Exception thrown when Django auth middleware is not used
issue_body:
**Scenario**
I am writing several Django based APIs that utilize Application Insights for logging. These APIs do not have Django's built-in authentication, so the authentication middleware is not used.

**The problem**
This package does not take this into account, so an exception is thrown when processing the request because `request.user` is not set.

**Solution**
I made the necessary code change to allow for this behavior. Here is the pull request: #58

(Meta: trying to do via a more structured spec process)

What & Why
===

**Problem**: The notebook exploration cell is generally too small for exploration, and the current process of opening the visualization in a new tab is slow and loses in-notebook state.

**High-Level Proposal**: Change the behavior of the 'pop out' button to cause the iframe to take up most of the width, height of the parent iframe. It should not disrupt the scroll state, nor scrollability, of the parent notebook/frame.

Tasks
===

* ☐ **Change icon**: `pop out` -> `maximize`
* ☐ **postMessage protocol, communication**: Manually implement parent/child frame communication. We expect the postmessage protocol to change as part of the graphistryjs interactive embedding api advances, but the usability benefit here outweighs waiting. 

* ☐ **Resize logic**: basic intent is to take most of the screen while still allowing scrolling between cells. So, the frame should be anchored on the same absolute Y position. To allow scrolling, the width will be `screen - 100px`, and centered. We may need to play some tricks on the DOM and CSS to make it do that.

* ☐ **Minimize logic**: the parent frame will ack expansion to the child frame, at which point the `maximize` icon switches to `minimize`, and click it restores the frame size

Out of Scope
===

* GraphistryJS-based implementation

Who
===
**Implementation**: @thibaudh @lmeyerov
**Design feedback**: @padentomasello 
**Implementation feedback**: @briantrice @quinnhj @trxcllnt 

Questions
===

* If the user really does want a new tab, how should they now achieve that? Keep the pop-out button, or some other way? One thought already: may be nice to expand the control options on the static loader screen.

* Should the resizing be an animated tween or immediate?

If I install `pybind11` into the virtualenv, the build does not fail that early. However, I can see that `pip` downloads a source distribution for `pybind11` again (even though it is already installed).

### What is wrong?

Add `PAYGAS` opcode to abstract away `gasprice`


### How can it be fixed

PAYGAS opcode takes one input `gasprice` off the stack and deduct `gasprice*tx.gas` from the contract's balance

### What is wrong?

We're missing developer docs on how to use CancelTokens

### How can it be fixed

Write some docs and example code showing how to use CancelTokens. https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/pull/445#discussion_r173408814 may be a good starting point

issue_title: [ENH] I miss the download size details conda previously provided
issue_body: I mentioned this to @kalefranz when I saw in a canary release that download-sizes had disappeared. I thought I was going to be able to live with it, but now I'm finding I'm frustrated by not having some idea of how long a conda environment creation is going to take.  When it takes long enough for me to notice/care (or potentially abort) it is always because of the number/size of files that need to get downloaded (this is especially true if I'm on a slow wifi connection in a coffee shop, plane, or pay-per-MB tethered on my phone).  I don't get that information any more and it makes me sad.

issue_title: `conda.install.update_prefix` is modifying cached pkgs in Windows
issue_body:
Since files are hardlinks, it looks like conda's prefix replacement mechanism is breaking its own files.

File contents inside package:

```python
x = r'/opt/anaconda1anaconda2anaconda3\Scripts',
```

File contents after installing in 'env1':

```python
x = r'C:\Miniconda\envs\env1\Scripts',
```

File contents after installing in 'NOTENV1':

```python
x = r'C:\Miniconda\envs\env1\Scripts',
```

Note that the second install fails, because the first one modified the cached files in `C:\Miniconda\pkgs`.

Reading @asmeurer comments in #679, I agree that the correct behavior in this case would be to delete the file being modified and re-create it to avoid this issue.

I'm trying to use Miniconda offline. During the process, I discovered that certain packages have trouble resolving.
If I were to download all of the transitive dependencies for `pandas` and put it in a custom channel, then I can easily do `conda install --offline --channel file:///path/to/channel pandas`. That will work without a problem.
However, if I try to do the same for `ipython`, I get an unresolved dependency. These are the exact reproduction steps:
1. Download all transitive dependencies of the latest `ipython` for linux-64.
2. Create a custom channel by placing all of the packages into a linux-64 folder.
3. Run `conda index` to create the channel.
4. Run `conda install --offline --channel file:///path/to/channel ipython`.

The response is:

```
Fetching package metadata: ..
Solving package specifications:
Error: Could not find some dependencies for pickleshare: path.py 7.6

Did you mean one of these?

    pickleshare

Did you mean one of these?

    path.py

You can search for this package on anaconda.org with

    anaconda search -t conda path.py 7.6

 (and similarly for the other packages)

You may need to install the anaconda-client command line client with

    conda install anaconda-client
```

``Traceback (most recent call last): File "./nb_author_id.py", line 15, in from email_preprocess import preprocess File "../tools/email_preprocess.py", line 6, in from sklearn import cross_validation File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/sklearn/init.py", line 57, in from .base import clone File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/sklearn/base.py", line 11, in from .utils.fixes import signature File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/sklearn/utils/init.py", line 10, in from .murmurhash import murmurhash3_32

I will say this seems to have come up with a few people. Uninstalling and reinstalling conda and then packages was my first port of call however that does not seem to have helped.

What jumps out to me straight away is that i am working out of a conda environment - /anaconda/envs/yo/lib/python2.7/site-packages - i would have thought that using ./myscript.py from the virtual environment should be using the packages and env from conda and not trying to go to my local site package directory?

Im not using ipython/jupyter and have been running ./myscripy.py directly from terminal!

This problem has been very frustrating - i did try and unset the python path before activating the virtual conda env but no luck and i'm hitting the same issue!

In the header of mpyscripy.py i do have #!/usr/bin/python - is this making my script default to the user local package site

I would be extremely appreciative if anyone can help me solve this issue!

issue_title: Can only use one md5 or url spec at a time
issue_body:
In implementing #6956, I realized you can only use one non-named spec (e.g. url or md5 spec) at a time.

So

    conda install '*[url=https://some/url/to/package.tar.bz2]' '*[md5=deadbeef]'

would collapse down to a single spec (i.e. one would be clobbered by the other) because of how `specs_map` is used in `conda/core/solve.py`.
