issue_title: Rasterio package broke after recent GDAL update
issue_body: looks like the recent updates to GDAL have broken the `rasterio`.  Installed rasterio from pypi DOES work with the most recent GDAL update.  I attempt to use `conda skeleton` to rebuild the recipe for rasterio but got build errors.

We set `conda-build/root-dir` at conda-forge so that we can share packages that are built with the Docker container and also set a place for them to upload. Using the most recent release of `4.2.11`, appears to not respect this.

issue_title: Nodes and Labels are not redrawn
issue_body: Hi, not sure if this is a bug in visjs or in the angular-visjs directive. When the zoom is applied and you add nodes to a dataset that is linked to the directive, the nodes are not redrawn until you move the mouse or zoom out. See this [Code](https://github.com/Tooa/angular-visjs-playground) for the problem. Just start the application with `http-server`. Do the following to reproduce the problem: 1. Zoom in. It's enough, when all labels are visible. 2. Click in the textbox (upper right corner) and press (and hold) the arrow up button. You will notice, that nodes are removed from the graph. Do this until the limit is reached (0.0009) This works pretty well! 3. Now press (and hold) the arrow down button. Problem observation: Nodes and labels disappear. Zoom out or mouse moving will redraw the graph. Why does this work for removing nodes, but not for adding nodes? Best Uli

issue_title: help text for missing subcommands is not helpful
issue_body:
`az vm usage
usage: az vm usage [-h] {list} ...
az vm usage: error: the following arguments are required: subcommand`

This error message is hard to grock: we tell users that they are missing the "subcommand" argument but there is no argument called subcommand, of course.  We should just tell them what subcommands *are available*.  I get that if you know a lot about the CLI you can discern that '{list}' is the subcommand, but there's no real way to figure that out based on the text

issue_title: Networking/Application Gateway API 2017-06-01 for Early July release
issue_body:
### Description
We are pushing to release few features for application gateway in the early July release for CLI.
[Swagger changes](https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs/pull/1285) are in for these features. 
**Note**: We are running late as NRP update is running late and hope that it gets over by 27 June.

### Summary of Changes
1)	ApplicationGateway Ssl Policy custom cipher suites feature [new properties added to Sslpolicy Property of ApplciationGatewayPropertiesFormat]
2)	Get AvailableSslOption feature [new resource ApplicationGatewayAvailableSslOptions and child resource ApplicationGatewayPredefinedPolicy]
3)	Redirect Configuration feature [new child resource ApplicationGatewayRedirectConfiguration for Application Gateway, new properties in UrlPathMap, PathRules and RequestRoutingRule]
4)	Azure Websites feature [new properties in ApplicationGatewayBackendHttpSettingsPropertiesFormat, ApplicationGatewayProbePropertiesFormat, schema for property ApplicationGatewayProbeHealthResponseMatch]
5)	Backendhealth nic fix [since powershell references NetworkInterfaceIPConfiguration instead of ResourceChild, returning SubResource for NetworkInterfaceIPConfiguration in Swagger created problem]

issue_title: Generated Python 3 modules has syntax errors, causing a fall back to Python 2 modules
issue_body:
I'm assuming recent changes have made the code generator output two versions of the code, one for Python 2 and another for Python 3.

However, the Python 3 generated code contains a recurring pattern where a parent class's __init__ method is called with a malformed parameter list starting with a comma (`__init__(, arg1, arg2, etc)`, causing a syntax error. Here are some examples:

https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/72d71b30af36e65b333e2225ce8732afa53a4ec4/azure-mgmt-monitor/azure/mgmt/monitor/models/rule_email_action_py3.py#L43

https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/72d71b30af36e65b333e2225ce8732afa53a4ec4/azure-mgmt-monitor/azure/mgmt/monitor/models/rule_webhook_action_py3.py#L42

https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/72d71b30af36e65b333e2225ce8732afa53a4ec4/azure-mgmt-monitor/azure/mgmt/monitor/models/diagnostic_settings_category_resource_py3.py#L46

The `__init__` module of `azure.mgmt.monitor.models` uses a massive try/except block to import all the `_py3` modules, and if a SyntaxError or ImportError is caught it imports the Python 2 modules instead.

I'm not privy to how the code generator works and may be missing something, but IMO a cleaner way to test the version of Python IMO would be to use an if statement like `import sys; if sys.version[0] >= '3':` instead of the try / except. That way automated testing could ensure that no import errors or syntax errors occur at all.

On a different note it's a pleasant surprise to see a major cloud provider supporting Python 3 type annotations in its SDK. :)

issue_title: Regression: Multi chunk transfer hangs as merging chunks fails
issue_body:
### Description

This is caused by https://github.com/Azure/azure-data-lake-store-python/commit/4b3171d87fe0efeadcd688a561b67144d229c288 which removed `_submit` to achieve better control of done callback behavior and avoid a race condition. 

However `_submit` was called when merging chunks and now fails.

The behavior is that the transfer will hang after the error. I'm not clear why it hangs instead of actually bubbling the error up (that may be due to a separate issue).

I'll submit a patch once I finish testing. Note that this affects latest release of the CLI.
```
azure.datalake.store.transfer : Chunks transferred
azure.datalake.store.transfer : Merging file: transferring
concurrent.futures : exception calling callback for <Future at 0x10faa1610 state=finished returned tuple>
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/clehene/work/azure-cli/env/lib/python2.7/site-packages/concurrent/futures/_base.py", line 301, in _invoke_callbacks
    callback(self)
  File "/Users/clehene/work/azure-cli/env/lib/python2.7/site-packages/azure/datalake/store/transfer.py", line 434, in _update
    merge_future = self._submit(
AttributeError: 'ADLTransferClient' object has no attribute '_submit'
```

Interrupting the process after the exception will "wake it":

```
^Cazure.datalake.store.transfer : <azure.datalake.store.transfer.ADLTransferClient object at 0x104fba290> suspended and persisted
azure.datalake.store.transfer : Shutting down worker threads
azure.datalake.store.transfer : Shutdown complete
Application event 'Application.TransformResults' with event data {'event_data': {'result': None}}
Application event 'Application.FilterResults' with event data {'event_data': {'result': None}}
```

---

### Reproduction Steps
* Upload a file and make sure the chunk size is smaller than the file size 

### Environment summary

**SDK Version:** What version of the SDK  are you using? (pip show azure-datalake-store)
```
Name: azure-datalake-store
Version: 0.0.15
```
If you specify `service_id` and publish a web service, it crashes because `service_id` cannot be imported.

issue_title: The way certifi is updated seems hard to maintain in production and is possibly a security risk
issue_body:
This is possibly a misunderstanding of mine, so please correct me if I'm wrong.

The CA bundle is updated in certifi simply by pushing a new version of certifi to github and pypi. This has a simple security consequence: Any Python code that uses certifi does not use the latest CA bundle until certifi is explicitly updated. Unlike traditional package updates, *all* (or almost all) certifi updates are by design security updates (and rarely feature updates), and these are pretty frequent: Once or twice a month. For other packages, security updates are pretty infrequent. Thus, security-conscious developers would want to update certifi with every new release.

Sometimes, this is not so simple. For instance, consider a Python web server that uses requests to make outside REST API calls and certifi to secure them. Such servers can be up for months or longer. Updating the certifi package would require the server (at least the Python part of it) to restart, which can be undesirable.

Another complication is caused by pip's `requirements.txt` format. Many Python hosts (for example, Heroku, or Amazon Elastic Beanstalk, or Google App Engine...) take a pip `requirements.txt` file along with your code and set up an environment using this file. The "recommended" way to create this file is to run `pip freeze`. Unfortunately, this command pins down the version of packages, so you could get something like `certifi==2016.8.8`, which is somewhat of a security risk - you would never get an up-to-date CA bundle.

Furthermore, even if you do change the `requirements.txt` line to something like `certifi` or `certifi>=2016.8.8`, if your environment is set up using `pip install -r requirements.txt` and it already had an old version of certifi installed, it would not get upgraded. It only gets upgraded if `pip install` explicitly gets the `-U` switch; there is no way to "force an upgrade" through the `requirements.txt` file alone.

I am not sure what the best way to remedy this would be, but I suggest decoupling the certifi version from the bundle version and having certifi try to auto-update the bundle every, say, 24 hours, from a reliable source like mkcert.org (using certificate pinning to enhance security and to avoid having a chicken and egg problem).

Thanks for a great package!

issue_title: Khatri product of matrices to Tensor
issue_body:
Hi,
I'm trying to decompose a Tensor (m, n, o) into matrices A(m, r), B (n, r) and C (k, r). This is known as  [PARAFAC decomposition](https://tensorly.github.io/stable/tensor_decomposition.html). [Tensorly](https://github.com/tensorly/tensorly) already does this kind of a decomposition. I'm trying to use autograd for such a decomposition. 

The process is simple:

1. start with random A, B, C
2. find the gradient wrt each and perform GD

For defining the cost function, we need to get a tensor (m, n, o) from A, B, C. This is known as Khatri product. Tensorly defines this as:

```python
 from functools import reduce
 def kt_to_tensor(A, B, C):
     factors = [A, B, C]
     for r in range(factors[0].shape[1]):
        vecs = np.ix_(*[u[:, r] for u in factors])
        if r:
           res += reduce(np.multiply, vecs)
       else:
           res = reduce(np.multiply, vecs)
    return res
```

I can define my cost as:

```python
def cost(A, B, C):
    pred = kt_to_tensor(A, B, C)
    error = (pred-t).flatten()
    return (error**2).mean()
```

However, computing the gradient (multigradient) over this would give the following error:

```python
NotImplementedError: Gradient of ix_ not yet implemented.
```

I then tried to make the function definition simpler, as follows:

```python
def new_kt_to_tensor(A, B, C):
    m, n, o = A.shape[0], B.shape[0], C.shape[0]
    out = np.zeros((m, n, o))
    k_max = A.shape[1]
    for alpha in range(0, m):
        for beta in range(0, n):
            for delta in range(0, o):
                for k in range(0, k_max):
                    out[alpha, beta, delta]=out[alpha, beta, delta]+ A[alpha, k]*B[beta, k]*C[delta, k]
    return out
````

However, gradient could not be computed over this too. I modified cost to use `new_kt_to_tensor` instead of `kt_to_tensor`. Error:

```python
AutogradHint: This error *might* be caused by assigning into arrays, which autograd doesn't support.
Sub-exception:
ValueError: setting an array element with a sequence.
```

Ofcourse, I had checked that both these function definitions return the exact same result.

```python
np.allclose(new_kt_to_tensor(A, B, C), kt_to_tensor(A, B, C))
True
```

I was wondering if you could let me know the best way to proceed with this usecase.

I have also asked [this StackOverflow question](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/43253670/khatri-product-of-matrices-using-np-tensordot) to try and get a `np.tensordot` based solution for this multiplication.

issue_title: Reports /bin/sh: 1: coverage: not found when using Debian packages.
issue_body:
https://travis-ci.org/cheako/Tor2web-1/builds/285180144#L1782

Debian installs coverage to python-coverage.  A command line option would be a simple solution, but ideally in shell one would do something like...
```
COVERAGE=false
[ "`type -t coverage`" ] && COVERAGE=coverage
[ "`type -t python-coverage`" ] && COVERAGE=python-coverage
[ "$COVERAGE" == false ] && echo "No coverage, all calls will fail."

$COVERAGE xml -i
```

The cluster ordinal is assigned as instances enter the running state. The ordinal is determined by an ordering on the composite key (launch time, private IP and instance ID) which is not necessarily consistent with the cluster ordinal.

We should include cluster name and cluster ordinal as the dominant members in the composite key of the ordinal ordering. The problem is that these two are derived from instance tags, not attributes of the boto.ec2.instance.Instance objects which is what the key function currently is limited to accessing. 

We could bind() the instances before sorting them. This might be expensive.

We could access the instance.tags dictionary in the key function. Some tags might not be populated.

issue_body:
I want to add Pindel to the exome pipeline, and one of the steps is creating a config for pindel with insert sizes of each of the bams.   I want to avoid adding a non-Docker dependency to the pipeline.

This command works great:
`samtools view -f2 $FILE | head -1000000 | awk '{s+=sqrt($9^2); i+=1} END {print s/i}'`

This command has several problems:
`sudo docker run --rm -v $(pwd):/data quay.io/ucsc_cgl/samtools view -f2 /data/pair9.tumor.bam | head -1000000 | awk '{s+=sqrt($9^2); i+=1} END {print s/i}'`

This gives me a broken pipe (and the correct answer). Samtools and docker.io continue to run, and no further invocations will work.  I can stop it with sudo docker stop $(sudo docker ps -a -q), but that will kill every container running on the instance and is a hacky solution to boot.

issue_title: B902 requires first parameter named self for __init__ of metaclass

issue_body:
The `ServerConfig` class does not currently require that the "version" attribute be provided. This is problematic when an entity must make an assumption about what version the server is running. For example, entities have different sets of fields based on what version the server is running, so of course an assumption must be made - but what version should be assumed?

Update method `ServerConfig.__init__`. If no version is passed in when instantiating a `ServerConfig` object, then some version should be assumed. I would support assuming version '1!0' (epoch 1, version 0). This version will be greater than any version provided by Satellite, thus letting us assume that a user is running a development version of Satellite by default. This will work so long as Satellite retains its current versioning scheme.

issue_title: Coordinates on grid are used inconsistently throughout the code
issue_body:
I am opening this issue as a general case of this earlier one: #191 that identifies a specific mix up in the height/width of the canvas. A [related question](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/projectmesa/QXjcXa4ijBU) was asked in the mailing list by Victor Norman on Dec 25th 2015.

- I noticed that the coordinates for `row, _row, height, y, _y` and `col, _col, width, x, _x` are used in the order `(a, b)` and `(b, a)` indiscriminately throughout the code.
- As shown above, the naming of these indices varies.

This leads to confusion and must be fixed. This is not an easy task.

My suggestion is to proceed in two steps:
- First, we need to rename the variables to `_row` and `_col` throughout the code, which may or may not break the examples, depending on how class attributes are accessed. This is a deep undertaking as, as far as I can tell, every single class is affected; I doubt we can do this one class at a time.
- Second, (that will break the examples, and should be in the milestone "The Rebirth"), is to rewrite the classes using consistent signatures for all methods. My suggestion here would be to use a `class Position` to encapsulate the indices; maybe we could use a named Tuple, or an extension of named Tuples?

Depending on the timeline of "The Rebirth", we might be able to skip the first step and concentrate our energies on the second step


Here are two examples extracted from #191:
**From Grid() in mesa.space.py:**
```
for row in range(self.height):
    for col in range(self.width):
        yield self.grid[row][col], col, row  # agent, x, y
 ---------------->>     Y    X     X    Y
```

**From `test_grid.py` :**
```
for agent in self.agents:
    x, y = agent.pos
    assert self.grid[y][x] == agent          # X, Y  <==>  Y, X
```

So we have this OCSP Stapling LTM profile, and interestingly it has the following required attributes:

proxyServerPool, dnsResolver, trustedCa

The problem is that 2 of them: 'proxyServerPool, dnsResolver' are mutually exclusive ONLY when another parameter 'useProxyServer' has a value of 'enabled'. When that value is set to 'disabled', both parameters can exist together. 

I was thinking in order to fix this we just make 'useProxyServer' as a required attribute, and stick 'proxyServerPool, dnsResolver' into ExclusiveAttributesMixin.

issue_title: Product alert emails should use the Dispatcher, and provide an HTML template
issue_body:
`customer.alerts.utils.send_product_alerts` could do with some refactoring to be more consistent with the rest of the code base. Specifically:

- Use `customer.utils.Dispatcher.send_direct_messages` to send the emails.
- Provide and use an HTML body template (currently it is plain text only, and method cannot be overridden)

I suspect the reason that `Dispatcher` wasn't used originally was because this method sends all the emails using a single SMTP connection. This use case is not unique and it might be better to let the various `Dispatcher` methods accept a `connection` which they can use if provided.

At the moment it's a dead-end.

I reckon you could point it at 54.77.234.211 (the box that latest.oscarcommerce.com points to)

issue_title: Extend basket view to use default shipping address
issue_body:
The basket summary view attempts to look up a default shipping method so an indicative order total can be displayed.  The checkout allows the method to be changed later on though.

At the basket stage, we don't know the user's chosen shipping address so we don't pass it.  However, Oscar does have a concept of default shipping address.  We should extend the basket view to pass the user's default shipping address (if one is known).

issue_title: Handle wildcards when trying to match the best server name
issue_body:
Currently if you have an apache vhost file with a `VirtualHost` containing:

```
  ServerName site.com
  ServerAlias *.site.com
```

And pass `-d site.com,www.site.com` to the Let's Encrypt client, you get a "We were unable to find a vhost with a ServerName or Address of www.site.com" error message and even worse get a selection table.

This is because [_find_best_vhost()] (https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/blob/master/letsencrypt-apache/letsencrypt_apache/configurator.py#L341) is completely unaware of aliases.

issue_title: Handling new requests for supersets of names in existing certs
issue_body: Right now we choose a random (i.e., first-mentioned by `os.listdir`) lineage to suggest as the lineage the user might want to renew, in the case where there are several lineages within which the requested cert could be an enlarged successor cert. Instead, we should suggest the *largest* relevant lineage.

issue_title: rrulestr Generates Datetime with Incorrect Daylight Savings Value
issue_body:
I'm using the dateutil 2.4.3 and encountering the following error using `rrulestr`:

```python
import pytz
from datetime import datetime
from dateutil.rrule import rrulestr

pacific_tz = pytz.timezone('America/Los_Angeles')
rrule_string = 'RRULE:FREQ=WEEKLY;COUNT=2;BYDAY=MO'
dt_start = pacific_tz.localize(datetime(2014, 10, 27, 9, 0))
```
 `list(rrulestr(rrule_string, dtstart=dt_start))` outputs the following:
```python
datetime.datetime(2014, 10, 27, 9, 0, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'America/Los_Angeles' PDT-1 day, 17:00:00 DST>), 
datetime.datetime(2014, 11, 3, 9, 0, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'America/Los_Angeles' PDT-1 day, 17:00:00 DST>)]
```

The second datetime is incorrect:

`datetime.datetime(2014, 11, 3, 9, 0, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'America/Los_Angeles' PDT-1 day, 17:00:00 DST>)` --> November 3rd 9AM should not be in DST.

The *correct* datetime would be:
```python
pytz.timezone('America/Los_Angeles').localize(datetime(2014, 11, 3, 9, 0))
datetime.datetime(2014, 11, 3, 9, 0, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'America/Los_Angeles' PST-1 day, 16:00:00 STD>)

```

issue_title: RFC: untangle lru_cache and test cache
issue_body:
The current lru_cache code is complicated because it mixes several intentions. Let's untangle them.

The two goals are 1) in-memory LRU memoization to lessen remote data fetches; 2) persistent caching, primarily so that tests do not require network access.

Consequences of mixing these concerns are:
* Can't use external lru_cache code (incl. in 3.x)
* Configuration is confusing. uta connect() requires a cache mode that's different than the lru_cache mode, and neither checks whether the supplied value is legit.
* Learning mode is slow (probably because it writes back every time).
* As implemented, the hdp interface is also entangled in caching.

I'm going to park this as a placeholder for discussion. I think I'd like to see us revert to a cache-unaware UTA module, and a (new) caching hdp that accepts an underlying hdp (eg uta) that merely caches data.

I'm using Flask's [`MethodView`s](http://flask.pocoo.org/docs/0.11/views/#method-based-dispatching) for an API.

Basically, the idea is to have one class per endpoint and one class method for each HTTP method. The method name is the HTTP method, lowercase.

```python
from flask.views import MethodView

class UserAPI(MethodView):

    def get(self):
        """User detail view.
        ---
        get:
            responses:
                200:
                    schema: UserSchema
        """
        users = User.query.all()
        ...

    def post(self):
        """User post.
        ---
        post:
            responses:
                201:
                    schema: UserSchema
        """
        user = User.from_form_data(request.form)
        ...

app.add_url_rule('/users/', view_func=UserAPI.as_view('users'))
```

AFAIU, there is currently no support for Flask's `MethodView` or Flask's `View`, only functions are supported. Is this correct?

I think I managed to get apispec to produce the correct OpenAPI file using the following patch:

https://github.com/marshmallow-code/apispec/compare/dev...Nobatek:dev_method_views

The idea is that if the `view` is a `MethodView`, we should not use the `view`'s docstring but loop over the docstrings of each method.

Thinking of it, maybe the only difference is that this allows the user to keep each docstring in each method rather than grouping all of them at the top of the class. This can be seen as cosmetic, but I think it would be better this way.

I'd be happy to get any feedback.

If there is interest for this feature, I may clean this up, add tests and all, but before I move any further, I'd rather be sure I'm on the right tracks.

issue_title: Should we use pyserial-asyncio or the aio built into pyserial?
issue_body: I see that [pyserial has "experimental asyncio support"](https://pythonhosted.org/pyserial/pyserial_api.html#module-serial.aio). Which is better to use—that or this project?

issue_title: Update the user guide about the GPU support
issue_body:
[The recent feature](https://github.com/apple/turicreate#version-50-beta) supports four models to utilize GPU automatically depending on the version of Mac OS. However, the user guide does not mention about the feature. Hope to update the guide to let users know whether the model can automatically utilize the GPU or not. 

- Currently, Image Classification, Image Similarity, Object Detection, and Activity Classification support the feature but only Image Classification mentions it. 
- The other three applications (Style Transfer, Recommender System, Text Classifier) does not mention whether they don't automatically detect the GPU.

issue_title: Recommender CoreML deployment instructions result in Library not loaded
issue_labels: [{'id': 964110671, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw5NjQxMTA2NzE=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/apple/turicreate/labels/1807', 'name': '1807', 'color': '02f73b', 'default': False}, {'id': 774366891, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw3NzQzNjY4OTE=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/apple/turicreate/labels/Core%20ML', 'name': 'Core ML', 'color': 'b3a9f2', 'default': False}, {'id': 975246878, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw5NzUyNDY4Nzg=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/apple/turicreate/labels/P1', 'name': 'P1', 'color': 'f90d09', 'default': False}, {'id': 775278782, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw3NzUyNzg3ODI=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/apple/turicreate/labels/docs', 'name': 'docs', 'color': 'edb423', 'default': False}, {'id': 962932099, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw5NjI5MzIwOTk=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/apple/turicreate/labels/recommender', 'name': 'recommender', 'color': 'a4d655', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
Following the instructions at https://github.com/apple/turicreate/blob/master/userguide/recommender/coreml-deployment.md results in something like:

```
dyld: Library not loaded: @rpath/libRecommender.dylib
  Referenced from: /var/containers/Bundle/Application/22AF80C7-D4E2-46F7-B23D-F560585BF32E/Recommend.app/Recommend
  Reason: image not found
```

The reason is that our instructions don't talk about including the dylib in both Linked Frameworks and Embedded Binaries. We need to make sure the library gets both linked against, and included in the app bundle.

issue_body:
The function is `numina.array.peaks.findpeaks1D.refinePeaks_spectrum`, that tries to fit a centroid to a peak using nearby points. 

It has two methods. In method=2, data is fitted to a Gaussian by taken log of Y and then fitting a parabola. If Y has negative values, then 

RuntimeWarning: invalid value encountered in log
  poly = np.polyfit(x_fit, np.log(y_fit), 2)

is raised

I'd like to upgrade the minimum supported numpy version to 1.10.1. This numpy version was released in Oct 2015.

Numpy 1.10 added two features that are useful to us:

1. nan support in ``assert_allclose``
2. scalar support in ``np.digitize``

We've hacked around 1. in the test suite for awhile now, but it would be great to remove the hacked code.

#429 proposes using ``np.digitize`` to replace manual binning in the ``perez`` function. I could hack around this too, but I'd rather bump the numpy spec.

Any objections to bumping the numpy spec?

What do people think about these new communication forums?

- pvlib tag on stackoverflow: http://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/pvlib
- pvlib python google group: https://groups.google.com/forum/#!forum/pvlib-python
- pvlib python slack organization: https://pvlib-python.slack.com/

Here are my thoughts:

First, all credit goes to @mikofski for pushing this forward and setting up some things for us to try! Thanks!

I am concerned that too many forums could be counter productive, but I think we're big enough now that we could benefit from more than GitHub issues. Watching the project on GitHub does allow people to stay up to date on every little thing in the pvlib python world, but that is way too much information for many people. Spreading the communication over multiple platforms lets people focus on what they really care about at the cost of making things more complicated and potentially diluting the viewership of any given item.

I think it would be nice if we would use GitHub for bug tracking and development discussions, people would ask usage questions on Stack Overflow, and we’d use a mailing list for new version announcements. 

I know that a few people have tried to ask pvlib questions on stack overflow in the past with varying degrees of success. I am going to start watching Mark's new pvlib tag. I don't have much experience with google groups, but at this point I prefer stackoverflow for usage questions. In any case, while we're getting started with stack overflow and/or google groups, I think that people should feel free to open and then close a GitHub issue that links to the new post.

On a related note, [Sibbell](https://sibbell.com/about/) is a nice tool that will send you an email when there are new releases of any of your starred repositories. 

I like Slack for quick, informal conversations in which I don’t have to stress about if I’m going to come across the wrong way in a public forum. I don't like the constant din of interruptions on Slack, though.

I also get requests for help via email from time to time. I don't like answering questions via email because only a handful of people have an opportunity to learn from the exchange.

I don’t know what the ideal approach is, but I think we should try something new and see if it catches on!

Finally, we should add some notes to the documentation once we come to a consensus.

issue_title: Use DeclarativeContainer and Factory together to get a specific provider
issue_body:
Hi,


Here is a container, the goal is to be able to group providers in a container, while still being able to have a factory to provide the right one to the end user.
```
from dependency_injector import providers, containers

class Numbers(containers.DeclarativeContainer):
	int = providers.Singleton(
		int,
		1234
	)
	float = providers.Singleton(
		float,
		1.2345
	)
```

1.
```
number_factory = providers.Callable(
	Numbers.providers.__getitem__,
	get_number_required_from_db()
)
```

vs

2.

```
number_factory = providers.FactoryAggregate(
	int=Numbers.int_provider, 
	float=Numbers.float_provider
)
```

In 1. it looks hacky but probably does the job.

In 2. I don't want to have each provider directly in the FactoryAggregate, that could make things messy if there are a lot of injections.
Also, I don't want to have them declared one by one, as in not grouped into a DeclarativeContainer.
2. involves repeating each keyword argument in the FactoryAggregate which is not great.

Is there a better way to do what I am trying to achieve, am I missing something?

Thank you

For senza files with sensitive parameters, those parameters would ideally be encrypted.

It is possible to use KMS encryption to avoid having those values as plain text in the senza definition file. This, however, means that those parameters must be decrypted before CloudFormation orchestration, and it will happen on a local machine running senza, or through lizzy.

The suggestion is to use the prefix `senza:kms:` to clearly identify these parameters.
For reasons of simplicity, those parameters should be in the `SenzaInfo` section.

Ex.:

```
SenzaInfo:
  SensitiveParameter: "senza:kms:skdfjg-sdFDGS23rwfkgjhsdfGSDGER034teFSD"

SenzaComponents:
(...)
      someParameter: {{SenzaInfo.SensitiveParameter}}
```

issue_title: Cannot Deploy to Domain Root, No Hosted Zones Found
issue_body:
We cannot to deploy to our production domain `solutions.zalando.com` due to the following error:

**ERROR: ExternalAppLoadBalancerMainDomain CREATE_FAILED: No hosted zones named zalando.com. found**

It seems senza assumes the domain follows the structure `subdomain`.`hosted_zone` and attempts to create route53 records in `hosted_zone`. This works fine in most cases, but when attempting to deploy to a domain that matches our hosted zone exactly (i.e. the root), it fails because `hosted_zone` is incorrectly set to `zalando.com`. This is consistent with @harti2006's comment on #314.

To confirm this is not just a problem with `solutions.zalando.com`, I tried deploying to the root of our other hosted zone `norris.zalan.do` with the same result.

Is there a way to deploy a stack at our domain root? The announcement of senza 2.0 listed this exact scenario as one of the reasons to move to the new A record setup, so I expect it should be possible.

More details:

- senza version: 2.0.149 
- [senza yaml used](https://github.bus.zalan.do/norris/norris-devops/blob/bugfix/28-change-solution-center-live-url/solution-center/solution-center-prod.yaml).

This was motivated by #1159 . Prior to this I had never looked very closely at Z3's unit tests. Doing so has revealed several problems that need addressing.

* The tests seem rely on SASSERT(). That's a no-op in non debug builds, right? That doesn't seem like a great idea because that means we don't know if the checks actually pass on a release build.
* src/test/hashtable.cpp seems to redefine SASSERT() and that implementation isn't particularly good because it doesn't halt execution with a non zero exit code which means we'll easily miss any failures here.
* Several of the tests have `#ifdef _WINDOWS` (e.g.` src/test/api.cpp`) guarding them with no obvious reason why. This means lots of things won't get tested on TravisCI because that uses Linux.

While I'm here I've also observed that we seem to be using a "homegrown" unit testing framework. I would suggest that part of the solution here is to use a well tested and widely used unit testing framework rather than rolling our own.

Example C++ unit testing frameworks include 

* [GoogleTest](https://github.com/google/googletest) I've used this one. It's also used by LLVM.
* [Boost.test](http://www.boost.org/doc/libs/1_49_0/libs/test/doc/html/index.html)
* [Catch](https://github.com/philsquared/Catch)

My preference would be GoogleTest but that's only because it's the only C++ unit test framework I've used before. Other suggestions are welcome.

I have the [following code](https://gist.github.com/GuerricChupin/ff17ab16fe1e4a9698c33aee76e2bc74) (if you are interested in why it exists, please read my [StackOverflow question](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/46604222/z3-giving-unexpected-result-with-the-pdr-engine)).

I use Z3 4.5.1 compiled from the master branch (and recompiled something like an hour ago) with g++ 6.3.0 on Ubuntu 17.04. 

 When calling `z3 -smt2 so.smt2`, I get:
```
sat
(and (Main!slice!1 false) (not (>= (:var 5) 0)) (Main!slice!1 true))
```

However if I call `z3 -smt2 so.smt2 fixedpoint.xform.inline_eager=false`, I get:

```
unsat
(define-fun Init ((x!0 Int)) Bool
  (>= x!0 10))
(define-fun Main ((x!0 Int) (x!1 Int) (x!2 Bool)) Bool
  (and x!2 (>= x!1 1)))
(define-fun Trans ((x!0 Int)
 (x!1 Int)
 (x!2 Bool)
 (x!3 Int)
 (x!4 Int)
 (x!5 Bool)) Bool
  (and (or (>= x!4 1) (not x!5)) (= x!5 (>= x!1 0))))
```

I think the second answer is the correct one. Regardless, I doubt that this parameter should change the result so drastically.

issue_title: CFG performance bottlenecks for large binaries
issue_body:
As discussed with @ltfish, this is just a memo of known CFG performance bottlenecks:

**1)** For binaries with lot of symbols, CFGFast has some performance bottlenecks:

The pending_entry lookup runs worst case in O(n), but entry_list_empty is called also O(n) times, so in worst case this will end up in O(n^2) complexity, causing a slow-down for binaries with lots functions:

```python
    def _entry_list_empty(self):

        if self._pending_entries:
            # look for an entry that comes from a function that must return
            # if we can find one, just use it
            entry_index = None
            for i, entry in enumerate(self._pending_entries):
                src_func_addr = entry.returning_source
                if src_func_addr is None or src_func_addr not in self.kb.functions:
                    continue
                function = self.kb.functions[src_func_addr]
                if function.returning is True:
                    entry_index = i
                    break

            if entry_index is not None:
                self._insert_entry(self._pending_entries[entry_index])
                del self._pending_entries[entry_index]
                return
```
To illustrate this behavior, on this graph, the x-axis represent subsequential `_entry_list_empty` calls, while the y-axis shows the number of iterations needed till a valid entry is found. The runtime is the area below the 'curve':
![entry_index](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/759365/25215624/fee08016-259e-11e7-8f40-adcdee695497.png)


**2)** In `make_functions` in `cfg_base`, this part of code triggers the slow-path of `get_any_node`, since most of the looked-up addresses are not present in the `_nodes_by_addr` dict. It can cause a major slow-down, if the number of nodes is around 500k.
```python
        secondary_function_nodes = set()
        # add all function chunks ("functions" that are not called from anywhere)
        for func_addr in tmp_functions:
            node = self.get_any_node(func_addr)
            if node is None:
                continue
            if node.addr not in blockaddr_to_function:
                secondary_function_nodes.add(node)
```

issue_title: os.uname() is not available on Windows
Does reading from SQL to pandas result in column names of sqlalchemy type
issue_body:
Hello, 

Take this example:

```
import odo
import pandas as pd
odo.odo('mssql+pyodbc://MyMSSQLServer::MyTable',
        'hdfstore://c:/workspace/MyHDFFile.h5::/MyTable', schema='dbo')

h = pd.HDFStore('c:/workspace/MyHDFFile.h5')
type(h.select('/data', 'index <= 1').MyField.name)
```

Thanks.

During a conversation with @peterbe, we realized there were several ways of managing requirements.

Obviously, we agreed on the main necessity: reproducibility and environments consistency. We want to control the dependencies versions going to production.

Currently, in Kinto we have:
* the top-level dependencies (with optional range) in `setup.py`
* the whole dependencies list and their versions in `requirements.txt`
* we deploy kinto using the constraints of `requirements.txt` to keep deps under control
* pyup that opens a PR when a new dependency is available (which runs the tests)

So far, so good.

What was discussed is the fact that:

* The requirements.txt constraints are not used to run the tests on TravisCI
* The requirements.txt constraints are not used to initialize the developer local virtualenv
* We don't edit requirements.txt constraints manually. We run `make build-requirements` during the release process (which basically runs `pip install + freeze` to take the latest versions in range of `setup.py`) and rely on unit-tests (NB. we have 100% coverage)

This leads to situations where:

* Newcomers will fail at installing their dev environment because of an undesired upgrade
* Undesired upgrades can block unrelated contributions.
  * Example:
  * A contributor pushes some documentation changes, no change to the code base
  * CI fails because a backward incompatible upgrade was released and makes the code fail
  * We open a PR to fix the compatible version range in `setup.py`, and merge.
  * We can then merge/rebase the documentation PR
* Some dependency upgrade was not never ran locally by any developer. The dependency upgrade was just tested in unit/integration tests, and deployed.

Alternatively we could:
* Use the requirements constraints in dev + CI
* Remove the  `make build-requirements` part and let individual developers (or pyup) upgrade dependencies by editing `requirements.txt` manually (or using something like `hashin`)

Thoughts?
make a list of methods that shouldn't changed.

issue_title: pass a func to pmap and pmap_mpi
issue_body:
motivation: pre-process Frame before passing to Cpptraj Action

```python
pt.pmap(pt.distance, traj, n_cores=3, iter_options=new_func)
```

here: `new_func` is a function that modify Frame. Frame would be passed to `new_func` before passing to `pt.distance`. This will make the parallel calculation is more abstract and easier to modify the coordinate.

 a proposed example:

```python
def new_autoimage(iterable):
    ...
    yield Frame

pt.pmap(pt.distance, traj, n_cores=8, iter_options=new_autoimage)
```

issue_title: Excluding fields using a ResourceAdapter with the exclude attribute doesn't work
issue_body:
I have a resource adapter that excludes a field just like the documentation suggested:
```python
import odin
from odin.codecs import json_codec

class MyResource(odin.Resource):
  foo = odin.StringField()
  bar = odin.StringField()

class MyResourceAdapter(odin.ResourceAdapter):
  exclude = ['foo']

print(json_codec.dumps(MyResourceAdapter(MyResource(foo='1', bar='1'))))
```
prints the following JSON:
```json
{
  "foo": "1",
  "bar": "1",
  "$": "__main__.MyResource"
}
```

Passing exclude as a constructor argument works as expected as proven by the following code snippet:
```python
print(json_codec.dumps(MyResourceAdapter(MyResource(foo='1', bar='1'), exclude=['foo'])))
```

prints the following JSON:
```json
{
  "bar": "1",
  "$": "__main__.MyResource"
}
```

This could be a documentation issue but I'm not sure what is the right usage of the API to fix that.

issue_title: test: "fuzzing" OpenThread to provide robustness and negative stress testing
issue_body:
We have good positive path test coverage of OpenThread now using `make check` and the CI test suite.

Perhaps the negative path test coverage of OpenThread could be built using "fuzzing" techniques, and some of the tools available in that space.

The GDS [blog](http://blog.gdssecurity.com/labs/2015/9/21/fuzzing-the-mbed-tls-library.html) discusses the technique, good open source tools to implement it, and even have examples applying the method to an instrumented mbedtls:

> "Fuzzing is a testing technique that feeds a program under inspection with random inputs and observes its behavior. The goal of fuzzing is to discover bugs in the inspected program."

issue_title: spinel flag packing order for SPINEL_PROP_THREAD_ON_MESH_NETS
Invalid MPL cache entry lifetime value
issue_body:
I've just spotted that OT sets `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_MPL_CACHE_ENTRY_LIFETIME` to **5** seconds.

Current specification defines it to **30** minutes (according to RFC7731), but this time may be reduced to **90** seconds (due to errata [SPEC-679](https://threadgroup.atlassian.net/browse/SPEC-679))

If we adjust our cache lifetime, I believe there is a need to store our MPL Sequence value in a persistent storage, as it is done for MLE and MAC frame counters (e.g. store after each 32 MPL transactions or so..).

Do you think we should implement it, and if yes, where should we store MPL sequence value:
	- add a new key of settings to store MPL sequence only e.g. `kKeyMplInfo` inside the module
	- put MPL sequence value into `kKeyNetworkInfo`  (may be hard to implement)
	- add a new key of settings to store IP6 protocol data (currently only MPL) e.g. `kKeyIp6Info` with function ` Ip6::Store()` similar to `Mle::Store()`

issue_title: Extremely Large Models Not Saving Properly
issue_body:
So I've been working on a very long meshed highway network with a vgg-18 perceptual loss to boot.  However, adding just a few more commands for a RGB to HSV conversion in the loss causes the whole thing to be able to train, but it totally crashes and burns during the forward pass of the trained network. I get lots of errors similar to [[Node: save_1/restore_slice_662 = RestoreSlice[dt=DT_FLOAT, preferred_shard=-1, _device="/job:localhost/replica:0/task:0/cpu:0"](_recv_save_1/Const_0, save_1/restore_slice_662/tensor_name, save_1/restore_slice_662/shape_and_slice)]]
E tensorflow/core/client/tensor_c_api.cc:485] Tensor name "block343/b" not found in checkpoint files model.tflearn with AdaGrad, same thing for Adam.

Turning it off fixes it, turning it on breaks it. Not sure why. Here's the offending code.

        hsv_content = shift_img
        hsv_content = hsv_content + tf.ones_like(hsv_content)
        hsv_content = tf.div(hsv_content, 2.)
        hsv_content = tf.squeeze(hsv_content)
        hsv_max = tf.reduce_max(hsv_content, 2)
        hsv_min = tf.reduce_min(hsv_content, 2)
        hsv_delta = hsv_max - hsv_min
        hsv_c_sat = tf.maximum(hsv_max, 1e-8)
        hsv_sat = tf.clip_by_value(hsv_delta/hsv_c_sat, 0., 1.)
        hsv_sat = tf.expand_dims(hsv_sat, 2)
        hsv_val = tf.expand_dims(hsv_max, 2)
        hsv_content = tf.concat(2, [hsv_sat, hsv_val])
        shape = hsv_content.get_shape().as_list()
        hsv_compare = tf.concat(2, [tf.ones([shape[0], shape[1], 1]), tf.ones([shape[0], shape[1], 1])])
        variation_loss = variation_weight * tf.reduce_sum(tf.square(hsv_compare - hsv_content))

It's not the most efficient way of doing things (Hello tf.ones_like), but for testing purposes it seems to be what I need, which is creating a mean squared loss from an all 1s distribution on the SV planes of the HSV map and the input image.

Training this on one image and running it doesn't cause issues.

Assuming that my checkpoint file is in a folder called checkpoints and it contains a float-type tensor, would this be the use case?

auto tensor = tensorflow::ops::Restore(root, {}, "checkpoints/checkpoint", DT_FLOAT);

issue_title: Feature: Renewing STS credentials without prompting for password again.
issue_body:
I have been trying out this command-line tool to move our AWS Users over to SAML, including their CLI usage. Unfortunately you always need to sign-in again after 1 hour. Some of our users run processes which take more than an hour to complete and need AWS access throughout the process.

AWS allows SAML users to stay signed in for [up to 12 hours](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/security/enable-your-federated-users-to-work-in-the-aws-management-console-for-up-to-12-hours/). Amazon STS still only allows the generated keys to last for up to 1 hour.

I would love if this command-line tool would allow me to stay signed in, such that I don't have to re-enter my password every hour.

I have two ideas for how to achieve this:

1. Introduce a command-line switch such as `--auto`, which keeps the script running, remembering the user password in memory, and automatically re-authenticating when the time is about to run out.
2. Grab the cookie which contains the token, and write it to the `~/.aws/config` file, and when running the command again, check if the token is still valid, and generate a new STS credential.

The two tactics might even be complementary, such that the `--auto` parameter makes the script wait until the credentials are about to expire, and then re-authenticates using the token in step 2.

I have not looked deeply into the options for solving this, but I might be able to create a Pull Request, I would just like to know what method would be preferred.

issue_title: Be consistent with the usage of command line prompts
issue_body:
This applies to the bash-like prompts, represented with a dollar sign `$`, and the python primary and secondary prompts represented, by `>>>` and `...` respectively.

Although an argument can be made about the impracticality of the presence of prompt symbols when copying and pasting examples, the prompt symbols do help to distinguish between the command and its output. The latter advantage has been judged important enough to chose to use prompt.

issue_title: Add ability to explictly specify Kubernetes cluster
issue_body:
Currently, `KubeCluster()` is hardcoded to launch Dask workers in the "current" Kubernetes cluster, this being either the cluster where the current process is running ("in-cluster config") or as a fall-back the default cluster in the kubeconfig file. As this may not be the right strategy for all use cases, it would be nice to be able to be able to optionally specify more explicitly which cluster to use. Here are the choices I currently see possible:

- in-cluster
- default from kubeconfig
- non-default cluster (other context) from kubeconfig
- other cluster (URL to Kubernetes API server + TLS certificate for connection... is anything else needed?)

issue_title: Argparse decorators are treating quoted flags as actual flags
issue_body:
In the argparse decorators, `parse_quoted_string()` is being called which strips quotes from all arguments before parsing them with argparse. Therefore something like "-s" will be seen as -s.

This behavior is consistent with a Python script using argparse on options sent from the shell (ex: bash) since they also remove quotes first. However it still feels wrong. When I removed the behavior, lots of unit tests fail so we are obviously relying on it.

@kotfu @tleonhardt @anselor 
Do you have any thoughts on this behavior?

I have a dataset with a feature that takes on only integer values (a performance rating, for example).  Since SMOTE generates data along a continuous vector between two points, if they had differing values of this feature, the resulting point has a non-integer value.  Using a decision tree, it quickly learns that these non-integer values are a good predictor of being in the minority class.  For example, I end up with tree splits like:  if performance <4 and then the next split is if performance > 3 to a leaf with all minority class data (all generated by SMOTE).

For example, a leaf of all SMOTE data when Performance Rating is 3 < x < 4.
![smote-nc](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/43162/18696663/ea0dd88a-7f88-11e6-8956-9a5310d9afbd.png)


In the original SMOTE paper, the authors suggested a SMOTE-NC for non-continuous data that would use the median of k-nearest neighbors for those non-continuous features.  Is there is will to implement this feature?  I suppose the user would need to pass the an index list of the non-continuous features over what is currently being passed to the algorithm.

issue_title: Recommended renewal strategy should not trigger rate limiting if the script is failing
issue_body:
Issue #3552 describes an issue where certbot would fetch a cert and then fail to save it. Because I'm running it twice a day ([as recommended](https://certbot.eff.org/all-instructions/)), I've hit the renewal frequency rate limit, so now I can't easily renew it, even though I've moved the missing directory.

This seems like it could be a wide class of problems: some error causes a new cert to be fetched, but a later error prevents it from being saved successfully. Even if certbot was modified to detect *this* issue before getting a new cert (the topic of #3552), It might be more broadly nice if the recommended usage and rate-limiting config were such that rate-limiting wouldn't be triggered by a constantly-failing script. This could mean changing the recommended usage ([source](https://github.com/certbot/website/blob/master/_scripts/instruction-widget/templates/getting-started/renewal.html) to alternating days or the rate-limiting to allow up to 15-20 certs a week. A third approach would be to maintain a count of renewal attempts, and automatically skip some renewal attempts if recent attempts have been failing. 

(The first two solutions would presumably be implemented in another repository, but between the third solution and the fact that #3552 is in this repo, I'm filing it here. Hopefully that's not too confusing.)

issue_title: Impossible to scroll in a page after opening a modal and navigating browser Back button
issue_body:
## Detailed Description

When a user has a modal open and goes back in history with the `Back` browser button, it is impossible to scroll up or down anymore.

It seems the `<body>` element keeps the `.fixed.modal-open` classes. They are responsible to apply a `overflow: hidden` property.

Several user flows:

1. Open admin
1. Open anomaly
1. Close anomaly modal
1. Press back in browser
1. Press back in browser, again
1. It is now impossible to scroll in the page

Or

1. Open admin
1. Open dataset
1. Open resource
1. Press back in browser
1. It is now impossible to scroll in the page

Basically, whenever a modal is open at some point _and_ a browser history goes back to this state and before, it is impossible to scroll in a page anymore.

## Context

When I open a modal , I want to be able to navigate in a page , so I can do whatever i have to do .

## Possible Implementation

I am unsure which component is responsible for adding the `fixed` class to the `<body>` element.
It seems the location history should be watched by the modal element and trigger a proper `modal:close` event, as on clicking on the `Close` button, pressing on the `ESC` key or clicking outside of the modal boundaries.

issue_title: User current block gas limit as transaction gas upper bound
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

At the moment in raiden we use the [Morden Gas Limit](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/master/raiden/settings.py#L13) as the gas upper bound of all transactions. That is suboptimal as the gas limit can be changed by the miners and in the mainnet is already at the time of writting quite difference. What's worse if for some reason the block gas limit goes below that no transactions will even work.

## Solution

Make sure that all transactions are provided with the actual block gas limit as the upper bound and the result of estimate Gas as the lower bound.


## Tasklist

- [ ] Use the current block's gas limit as the upper bound of a transaction's gas.

Save and restart the node at the latest known valid state (#205)

- Save each state transition into the log prior to applying it.
  - Each transition must have a serial identifier.
  - This includes:
    - current mediated transfers states
    - all channels known balance proof (latest message, locks, and secrets)
- Save a snapshot of resulting state after the state transition is applied.
  - The snapshot must have an accompanying identifier for the latest transition applied
- Recover the state of the node from the log files.

## Problem Definition

The channel settle call was performed, and succeeded:

```
raiden_1  | 2018-07-13 13:39:46 [debug    ] STATE CHANGE                   [raiden.raiden_service] node=e0902284 state_change=<Block 3627480>                                                                       
raiden_1  | 2018-07-13 13:39:46 [debug    ] RAIDEN EVENT                   [raiden.raiden_service] node=e0902284 raiden_event=<ContractSendChannelSettle channel:6b44e524 token_network:7692624c>  
raiden_1  | 2018-07-13 13:39:46 [info     ] settle called                  [raiden.network.proxies.token_network] locked_amount=0 locksroot=0x node=e0902284 partner=22222222 partner_locked_amount=0 partner_locksroot=0x partner_transferred_amount=0 token_network=7692624c transferred_amount=0
...
raiden_1  | 2018-07-13 13:46:54 [info     ] settle successful              [raiden.network.proxies.token_network] locked_amount=0 locksroot=0x node=e0902284 partner=22222222 partner_locked_amount=0 partner_locksroot=0x partner_transferred_amount=0 token_network=7692624c transferred_amount=0                                                                                                                                      
web3_1    | 2018-07-13 13:46:54 UTC Transaction mined (hash 0xa4ba16c7adc8f49833cc333c71c66ba25fe6988c6cc79d501940b990165dfcb8)
```
but no state changes to update the channel state showed up in the logs, and even after a restart (which tried to settle the channel again, but failed because it's already settled), the channel is stuck in the `waiting_for_settle` state, and on the channels list.

issue_title: Add authentication for the Delivered message
issue_body:
We don't want to sign all messages because `ecrecover` is an expensive operation, specially we don't want to sign the `Delivered` messages because that would double the number of signatures, without encryption that means that an `Delivered` message can be easily forged.

A forged `Delivered`'s can be used to make a channel unusable and maybe to steal some asset.
- To make a channels unusable an `Delivered` for a `DirectTransfer` or `Secret` message is sufficient, since the sender would update it's state while the partner won't.
- To steal some asset a MediatedTransfer is required, `ABC`, **B** would need to intercept the `RevealSecret` messages and make sure that **C** don't receive it until the the `BC` lock expires, then it can settle the transaction `AB` on chain with the secret learned from the intercepted message (#473).
   - This is still possible with the Secret Registry **iff** the lock expiration changes, and this is possible since the settle timeout is not enforced to be constant through out the network

With an encryption scheme faster than `ecrecover`able signatures we can drop the signatures from the messages that are not exposed to the smart contract (messages that are signed only to inform us about the sender, eg. SecretRequest, TransferTimeout, etc.)

issue_title: Resume sending protocols message if a node is back at reachable state
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

The protocol implementation assumes that after [5 seconds](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/b70cd3d5074ed4605b3a643834b74dc5c5604ed7/raiden/network/protocol.py#L66-L67) the partner node is unreachable and drop the message, since Raiden doesn't have messages to request a re-sync this will force the nodes to settle on-chain (given that there is a difference in the internal node states)

## Solution

Instead of dropping the messages the protocol must keep all the unacknowledged messages and resume sending them if the node restores the reachable state, given that the corresponding channel is not closed.

## Related issues

Introduce network state for partner nodes #447
Randomly health check unreachable nodes #448

## Tasklist

- [x] Change the [greenlet task that sends protocol messages](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/b70cd3d5074ed4605b3a643834b74dc5c5604ed7/raiden/network/protocol.py#L114)
   - [x] Integrate the send task with the health check (note:  #448 and #247)
   - [x] Hold the unacknowledged messages (instead of dropping them)
   - [ ] Resume the message queue once the partner is reachable and if the the channel is **not** closed, this **must** be *per* channel

## Problem Definition

For the Developer's preview we have made Raiden Restartable. Which means it can resume after a graceful shutdown. But it can't recover after a sudden shutdown such as a powercut.

## Dependencies

Depends on the following issues:

- ~Make the channel use a state machine~ ( https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/issues/507)
- ~Write channel state changes in the WAL~ (https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/issues/684)
- Take a snapshot of the entire state at specific intervals (https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/issues/682)
- Recover from the WAL (https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/issues/592)

## Description

Save raiden's state into disk so that the process can be safely restarted and continue from the last known valid state after a failure (eg. power failure).

## Solution

Goals:
- All the tasks must expose it's current state to allow serialization of the node state.
  - Current use of greenlet tasks can make important data "unreachable" because it's held in the stack.
- Save state of all the existing channels (latest balance proof and known secrets).
- Operations that change the node state need to be serialized on disk prior to being applied (related to: #69).
  - Employ a journaling strategy (write-ahead-log) for state trasitions.
  - Use snapshots for fast recovery

Subgoals:
- Improve testability

## Tasklist

- [x] Define a general architecture to define state transition that can be easily saved on disk #299 (Implemented by PR #266)
- [ ] Save the state in disk
  - [x] state transition into a WAL log #298
  - [ ] save snapshots in disk #682
- [x] Restartable Mediated Transfers  #301 (implemented by PR #413)
  - [x] send state transitions to the raiden architecture #302
- [x] Update the tasks route state
  - [x] Add new available routes #456
  - [x] Remove routes that may not be used anymore #468
- [x] Restartable Channel Graph
  - [x] Save the data about the nodes that cannot be recovered from the block chain in the disk
- [x] Restartable Channels
- [ ] Restartable Token Swaps #303

issue_title: Rerun of completed tasks with missing output
issue_body:
A seeming simple scenario: Run a script with task, task completed. Remove output files, re-run, and sos will find that task already completed. In this case,

1. `sos run -s default` will still check signature and reaches the `task` statement because of signature mismatch.
2. underlying `sos execute task -s default` will refuse to run a completed task.
3. `sos run -s force` will rerun the step and task, but innocent steps/tasks will also be rerun.

This is a new problem with the new task format because previously sos has a `signature-mismatch` status for tasks. That is to say, when we check if a task is completed, it will check if the signature matches, so `sos execute task -s default` will execute if the signature mismatch.

Now, before we restore this `signature-mismatch` status, we need to think what this status means.

This status essentially binds the `task` file with its outputs, so a task would be considered failed whenever the input or output files are moved or removed. Considering you have a large number of jobs on the cluster, it is almost inevitable that input output files will be moved around after jobs are completed. 

There are also technical difficulties in getting this status. Because input and output files are stored outside of task files. We will have to read a task file, get its signature, and check input output files, potentially even calculate md5 of all these files. Considering doing this for ten thousand jobs that you have done in the past month each time you check the status of jobs? We can of course cache the status but we still need to check a lot of files to at least see if their timestamp has been change between each `sos status` check.

Now related to #1015 if we are re-executing a step with lots of jobs, jobs with now removed output files will be rerun, which is not acceptable in many cases.

So we are somehow stuck here with #1015...

Are factory.django.DjangoModelFactory fixtures in conftest.py available across applications?
issue_body:
https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest-django/issues/508

I'm using factory_boy with https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest-django.

pytest-django: 3.1.2
factory-boy: 2.8.1  (downgraded due to https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest-factoryboy/issues/47#issuecomment-318951957)
django: 1.11.4

Here's my conftest.py:

```python
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
import factory
import pytest
from django.contrib.auth import get_user_model
from django.core.management import call_command
from pytest_factoryboy import register

class UserFactory(factory.django.DjangoModelFactory):
    class Meta:
        model = get_user_model()

    username = factory.Sequence(lambda n: "user%03d" % n)
    password = factory.Sequence(lambda n: "pass%03d" % n)

register(UserFactory)
```

This makes ``user`` available as a fixture for me. However, it only works in tests in the same application, not project-wide (in different django application test directories).

Am I doing it wrong, then? I'm trying to narrow down if this is a mistake on my end, a bug, a potential feature, or expected behavior.

Currently setting up a station containing a qdac is very slow.

This is due to getting voltage, voltage_range, current, and current_range for all channels. The time spend seems to be evenly split between these 4 parameters. There are at least to ways of speeding this up. 

* Make the current related parameters optional (and probably off by default). Should give a factor of 2
* Don't ask for voltages for each channel individually. The QDaC can return all voltages in one command which is much faster. This may be fast enough to not need the removal of currents above. 
There is already partial support for this in the channelised version of the QDac driver when asking for more than one channel voltage but it needs to be integrated into the snapshot functions.

A minimal example is included below. The `DataSet.complete` returns while the loop is still running.
```
import time
import numpy as np
import qcodes
from qcodes.tests.data_mocks import DataSet2D
from qcodes.tests.instrument_mocks import DummyInstrument

dataset = DataSet2D()
A = DummyInstrument(server_name='test')
sweepvalues=A.dac2[0:100:.05]

#%%
#%Loop in background
data=qcodes.Loop(sweepvalues, progress_interval=.2, delay=.001).each(A.dac1).run(background=True)
print('sync %s' % data.sync())
print('fraction %.3f' % data.fraction_complete())
data.complete() # should wait on data to be ready

for ii in range(20):    
    data.sync()
    print('sync %s' % data.sync())
    print('fraction %.3f' % data.fraction_complete())
    time.sleep(0.2)
```

issue_title: Tasks that arrive before global scheduler receives any heartbeats never get scheduled.
issue_body:
If the global scheduler receives a task before it receives any heartbeats from the local schedulers, it will not be able to schedule that task (because there will be no local schedulers that satisfy the task's resource requirements).

This may be the source of hanging in some test cases, e.g., `testPassingInfoToAllWorkers` in https://travis-ci.org/ray-project/ray/jobs/200210187.

issue_title: Slower performance when passing numpy array into task versus list.
issue_body:
```python
import numpy as np
import ray
import time

ray.init()

@ray.remote
class Foo(object):
  def method(self, x):
      return x

a = Foo.remote()
x = np.random.rand(10, 10).tolist()

time.sleep(1)  # Wait for the actor to start.

start = time.time()
for i in range(1000):
    ray.get(a.method.remote(x))
print("Using list: ", time.time() - start)

x = np.random.rand(10, 10)

start = time.time()
for i in range(1000):
    ray.get(a.method.remote(x))
print("Using numpy array: ", time.time() - start)
```

On my laptop, this prints

```
Using list:  0.6015908718109131
Using numpy array:  0.895500898361206
```

The numpy array case is slower (presumably because the array does not get inlined in the task specification and goes through the object store instead).

**Proposal:**
1. Allow small numpy arrays to be inlined in the tasks.
2. Allow larger things to be inlined in the tasks.

**Potential Issues:**
1. The bigger the tasks are, the sooner Redis will run out of memory (until we are flushing keys from Redis).

cc @jsuarez5341

issue_title: client.join() with timeout throws an exception whether the timeout was reached or not
issue_body:
__Steps to reproduce:__
Run the following snippet, after assigning values to `host_lst`, `username` and `password` variables
```python
from pssh.pssh2_client import ParallelSSHClient

client = ParallelSSHClient(hosts=host_lst, user=username password=password)
cmd = client.run_command('ls -al')
client.join(cmd, timeout=60)
```
2. The following exception is being thrown:
```python
.../python2.7/site-packages/pssh/pssh2_client.pyc in join(self, output, consume_output, timeout)
    220                 raise Timeout(
    221                     "Timeout of %s sec(s) reached on host %s with command "
--> 222                     "still running", timeout, host)
    223             elif consume_output:
    224                 for line in output[host].stdout:

Timeout: ('Timeout of %s sec(s) reached on host %s with command still running', 60, '192.168.220.63')
```
__Expected behaviour:__
No exception was supposed to be thrown as the command completes successfully in the allotted time

__Actual behaviour:__ 
`Timeout` exception was thrown

__Additional info:__ 
From what I see the bug results from the following `if` statement (line 219 in pssh2_client.py):
```python
if timeout and not output[host].channel.eof():
...
```
As far as I see, `output[host].channel.eof()` is `False` when the command finishes to run thus the condition evaluates as `True` and an exception is thrown.
The only time that I see that `output[host].channel.eof()` is `True` is after the output was consumed.

issue_title: Bridge GO/AmiGO and Monarch evidence models
issue_body:
* AmiGO evidence solr schema: https://github.com/geneontology/amigo/blob/6165f094fd3770c731b517ca754edfb537e9295e/metadata/ann-config.yaml#L299-L342
 * Monarch example evidence model: https://github.com/monarch-initiative/monarch-app/blob/e423d75358c9b06bb7b370da47bf6c623e1b8347/conf/golr-views/single-tab/disease-phenotype.yaml#L130-L156

would be good to have a bit more documentation on each (@kltm and @kshefchek respectively)

The Monarch concept of an evidence graph generalizes the GO GAF evidence model. The latter allows only one link in a chain. This is actually a frequent issue for GO, where we have long needed to represent chains of two or more pieces of evidence. This is also unsatisfactory when we collapse a GOCAM to a GPAD, we have to find the one link the curator finds most pertinent (cc @balhoff). If GPADs had supported chains from the beginning this would be easier. The Monarch concept of an evidence graph generalizes chains further allowing arbitrary graphs connecting a source/subject to an object/sink (though in practice many such chains are links of length 2).

The Monarch evidence graph is represented as a bbop graph, which is stored as a string in solr. For example [this query](https://solr.monarchinitiative.org/solr/golr/select/?defType=edismax&qt=standard&indent=on&wt=json&rows=25&start=0&fl=*,score&facet=true&facet.mincount=1&facet.sort=count&json.nl=arrarr&facet.limit=25&facet.method=enum&fq=object_category:%22disease%22&fq=subject_category:%22gene%22&fq=subject_closure:%22HGNC:11604%22&facet.field=subject_taxon_label&q=*:*&packet=1&callback_type=search&json.wrf=jQuery321021151349255552598_1510954410222&_=1510954410224)

see the link between TBX5 and atrial fibrillation. This is actually *inferred* from the asserted graph below:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/50745/32971242-1e05900a-cba1-11e7-82a5-0bb676d42394.png)

As can be seen there are 2 links in the chain of inference. Only one has evidence asserted (the link between the variant and the gene is taken as true here).

in addition to the full evidence graph, the [monarch solr schema pattern](https://github.com/monarch-initiative/monarch-app/blob/e423d75358c9b06bb7b370da47bf6c623e1b8347/conf/golr-views/single-tab/disease-phenotype.yaml#L130-L156
) has convenience fields that list all the nodes in the evidence graph

```
"evidence_object": [
"MONARCH:e95f810c91005264",
"PMID:28416818",
"dbSNP:rs883079",
"ECO:0000213",
"PMID:28416822",
"EFO:0000275",
"NCBIGene:6910"
],
"evidence_object_label": [
"rs883079-C",
"MONARCH:e95f810c91005264",
"atrial fibrillation",
"PMID:28416818",
"TBX5",
"PMID:28416822",
"combinatorial evidence used in automatic assertion"
],```

there isn't a convenience field specifically for the ECO class, this could be extracted formally from the graph using RO:0002558, or hackily by looking at the evidence_object list and taking the ECO: class. This would map to the single 'evidence' field using in AmiGO-GOlr.

issue_title: Work around openssl's weird SSL_shutdown semantics by calling SSL_read during shutdown
issue_body:
So apparently openssl's `SSL_shutdown` semantics are even more convoluted than I realized:

https://bugs.python.org/issue30437#msg317028
https://github.com/openssl/openssl/issues/6262#issuecomment-389987860

There are two issues, which interact:

When `SSL_shutdown` is called a second time to read from the socket looking for a `close_notify` from the peer, it actually errors out if it sees something that *isn't* `close_notify`. Which of course can happen, because the other side could be doing anything, we don't know. And when this happens, the error reported is "syscall error", which makes no sense but [at least explains this](https://github.com/python-trio/trio/blob/82ec7c7532004237054ae511dd28d571a80f1c89/trio/_ssl.py#L768-L776) ([also](https://bugs.python.org/issue30437)) Apparently the recommendation in this case is to call `SSL_read` to drain the socket of actual data / other control messages, until it reports EOF (and then you know you got a `close_notify`).

In TLS 1.3, session tickets are sent from the server to the client as control mesages right after the handshake completes. This means that if a client connects, sends some data, and then disconnects without calling `SSL_read`, they never get the session tickets. If you then try to do a full clean shutdown, this means you *will* trigger the `SSL_shutdown` issue, and need to call `SSL_read` to clear it, so it's relevant to the above.

But, it also means that you kind of want to call `SSL_read` always, because reading session tickets is important – it lets you save a round-trip on your next handshake. ...maybe this should be a separate issue? I'm still confused and don't anticipate dealing with any of this immediately so I'm going to leave it here for now. Anyway, what this means is that maybe before shutting down your TLS connection you always want to call `SSL_read` a few times, just while there's data to process, so you don't miss reading session tickets. And fixing the full-`SSL_shutdown` case as described above won't actually help with this, because in normal usage we only ever call `SSL_shutdown` once.

But annoyingly, we can't do this automatically, or at all really, because of the requirement to *only read the data that's already arrived, without blocking*. The `Stream` interface doesn't have a `receive_some_nowait` method, and that's what `SSLStream` would need to call.

issue_title: Default `requests` timeouts are too short and can't be configured easily
issue_body:
The default is 10s for `read` and 60s for `connect`, neither of which makes much sense considering that the server is going to be an AWS API Gateway which has a timeout of 30s max. We should default to 40s for read (so the server has a chance to send the 504 back in case it times out) for read and 10s for connect. I don't see why a longer connect timeout is necessary.

This should also be easier to configure. I would think via environment variables so it can be changed at runtime and hanging the default timeout off the client instance rather than `_ClientMethodFactor`.

issue_title: Make launchers classes to be consistent
issue_body:
Currently we specify launchers by name to the provider, whereas it would be more elegant and consistent to have the launchers be classes. Launchers could then be specified in the config by passing a launcher object. We should have this added for 0.6.0 since we are making broad changes to both parsl and libsubmit.

The launcher classes should have a `__call__` function which can be invoked by the provider. This would allow for an additional check to be made to confirm that the object passed to the config is in fact callable.

issue_title: Error when creating HTTPS SNS Subscription
issue_body:
<!-- Love localstack? Please consider supporting our collective:
👉  https://opencollective.com/localstack/donate -->

The code looks like this (both for v3 and v4):

```python
  def merge(self, merge_commit_message=None,
              should_remove_source_branch=False,
              merged_when_build_succeeds=False,
              **kwargs):
        """Accept the merge request. """
...
```

The issue is that when you call: 

```
mr.merge(merged_when_build_succeeds=True)
```

The MR is merged immediately, _without_ waiting for the pipeline to finish, which is not what is expected.

The problem is that he argument is called `merge_when_build_succeeds` (note: no `ed`  at the end of `merge`) in `v3` and `merge_when_pipeline_succeeds` in `v4`.

Good news is that the fix is trivial:

```python
gl = gitlab.Gitlab("http://example.com", 'token', api_version=4)
project = gl.projects.get("foo/bar")
mr = project.mergerequests.list(iid=42)[0]
mr.merge(merge_when_pipeline_succeeds=True)
```

I can make a pull request in the documentation if you like until there is a proper fix

issue_title: Potential security issue with username/password authentication
issue_body:
## Description of the problem, including code/CLI snippet

I've written a custom login function to start a new session using username and password (my code is largely inspired from a snippet I found recommended somewhere). 
When I use it with wrong credentials I can still access some data, can you confirm whether or not that's intended? 

Here is my function:

```
import re
import requests
from gitlab import Gitlab

URL="<My server URL>"
SIGN_IN_URL = URL + "/users/sign_in"

def login_to_gitlab(username, password):

    with requests.Session() as session:

        token = None
        sign_in_page = session.get(SIGN_IN_URL).content
        for l in sign_in_page.split(b'\n'):
            m = re.search('name="authenticity_token" value="([^"]+)"', l.decode())
            if m:
                token = m.group(1)
                break

        data = {'user[login]': username,
                'user[password]': password,
                'authenticity_token': token}

        session.post(SIGN_IN_URL, data=data)
        gl = Gitlab(URL, api_version=4, session=session)

        return gl
```

If I call the following with wrong credentials:

```
gl = login_to_gitlab("fake_user", "fake_password")
``` 
I get access to:

```
# Getting any user by ID
user = gl.users.get(1)
# Getting any user by username
user = gl.users.get(username="an_existing_username")
# Get attributes from user
user.attributes
```
From what I've tried, I cannot list all projects, users etc.. but at one could list names of all users by iterating over ids:

```
for i in range(100):
    try:
       user = gl.users.get(i)
       print(user.attributes["name"])
    except:
       pass
```

If it's not expected behaviour, is it:
- an issue from my gitlab server setup? If that's the case, any idea what I should look for?
- an issue with the API?

If it is expected behaviour, any idea how I can confirm that the login worked or throw an error if it did not?

## Specifications

  - python-gitlab version: 1.3.0
  - API version you are using (v3/v4): v4
  - Gitlab server version (or gitlab.com): 11.0.0

issue_title: sudo should be remembered for SaveMixin objects
issue_body:
## Description of the problem, including code/CLI snippet
```python
grp = gl.groups.get('example-group')

notif = grp.notificationsettings.get(sudo=otheruser)
print("Old level:", notif.level)

notif.level = gitlab.const.NOTIFICATION_LEVEL_GLOBAL
notif.save()    # missing sudo=otheruser

notif = grp.notificationsettings.get(sudo=otheruser)
print("New level:", notif.level)
```

## Expected Behavior
I would expect this to change the notification level for the sudo'd user.

## Actual Behavior
When I call `notif.save()`, it has seemingly no effect, because I am missing `sudo`.

Instead, the changes were applied *to my admin account*!

Changing the code to `notif.save(sudo=otheruser)` works as expected.

## Specifications

  - python-gitlab version: **1.4.0**
  - API version you are using (v3/v4): **v4**
  - Gitlab server version (or gitlab.com): **10.6.4**

## Proposal
If an object is acquired using `sudo=user`, perhaps the library can *attach* that `sudo` user to the `SaveMixin` object, and automatically apply it when `.save()` is called without arguments.

I consider this a bug, and I can't imagine that anyone is *actually* relying on the current behavior.

## Description of the problem, including code/CLI snippet
```
services = gl.project_services.available()
```
is throwing an AttributeError
```
AttributeError: 'Gitlab' object has no attribute 'project_services'
```

From the documentation: http://python-gitlab.readthedocs.io/en/latest/gl_objects/projects.html#exammples

## Expected Behavior
All strings of available GitLab services are listed. Admin token is used.

## Actual Behavior
AttributeError is thrown. Looks like this feature is not implemented, yet?

## Specifications

  - python-gitlab version: 1.3.0
  - API version you are using (v3/v4): v4
  - Gitlab server version (or gitlab.com): Own GitLab server running 10.6.3

Both the 2.0.11 and 2.0.10 tarball packages (the only ones I checked) available for download are missing the MANIFEST.in file. This is problematic because it means that running "python setup.py sdist" on the source tree generated by one of these tarballs will create a broken package, missing buildutils.py, setupegg.py, and zmqversion.py at the top of the source tree alongside setup.py.

I'm trying to use pypi2rpm (http://pypi.python.org/pypi/pypi2rpm) to generate pyzmq RPMs that can be used in our production environment, but it doesn't work b/c it's using sdist as described above and is thus generating broken packages. Having scripts that download packages from PyPi and then repackage them for a particular environment seems to me a reasonable use case, making sure that MANIFEST.in is included in your packages would make this work again.

Thanks!
